− Iteration 1 — Avg Score: 81.1
+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 79.5
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The transfer vessel's air filtration system hummed with a persistent mechanical drone that reminded Arthur Neegan of the ore processors back on Mars—though considerably more refined. He sat in what they'd called the "guest quarters," a euphemism for a comfortable cell with no manual door controls. Through the porthole, Mars had shrunk to a rust-colored marble against the star-pricked void. Three days out from Chryse Station, and his escorts had maintained their professional silence. The Antares Diplomatic Corps insignia on their uniforms might have fooled someone who hadn't spent fifteen years in the Outer Colonies. Arthur knew better. Diplomats didn't carry military-grade stunners or move with the practiced efficiency of combat veterans. The door chimed and slid open. A woman entered—tall, dark-skinned, with the kind of augmented musculature that spoke of high-gravity world origins. Her uniform bore no insignia at all. "Mr. Neegan. We'll be docking within the hour. Someone would like to speak with you." "I gathered as much when your people showed up with weapons at my hab-dome." Arthur kept his voice steady. "Though I'm curious why anyone would go to this trouble over a mining claim." She smiled thinly. "You're an educated man, Mr. Neegan. First-class engineering degree from Luna Tech. You know perfectly well this isn't about mining rights." The docking was smooth—smoother than any commercial liner Arthur had experienced. They emerged into a private bay aboard what appeared to be a modified research station, though the heavy armor plating suggested other purposes. His escort led him through corridors lined with holographic star charts showing trade routes, mineral deposits, and tactical positions across the Inner and Outer Systems. The conference room was spartanly furnished: a single table, three chairs, and a wall-length viewport showing the distant Sun. A man sat facing the stars, his back to them. When he turned, Arthur recognized the sharp features and prematurely gray temples from countless newsfeeds. "Director Chen," Arthur said. "I should have guessed the Science Directorate would be involved." Chen Weixing, Deputy Director of the Solar Science Directorate, gestured to a chair. "Please, sit. We have much to discuss and little time to do it." He waited until the woman had left before continuing. "Tell me, Mr. Neegan, what do you know about Azra's properties?" "What any mining engineer knows. It accelerates radioactive decay. Properly channeled, it can turn a fusion drive into something approaching theoretical maximum efficiency. Improperly handled, it can turn a city block into radioactive glass." "A textbook answer." Chen touched a control, and the star chart transformed into a molecular diagram. "What the textbooks don't mention is that Azra's acceleration effect isn't linear. Under specific conditions—conditions we're still mapping—it can create cascade reactions that violate several fundamental assumptions about nuclear physics." Arthur studied the diagram. "You're talking about controlled chain reactions at the subatomic level. That's—" "Impossible? So was faster-than-light communication until we discovered tachyon manipulation." Chen leaned forward. "Your claim, Mr. Neegan, sits atop approximately forty-seven million metric tons of high-grade Azra ore. That's more than the combined output of all other Martian operations." "I had my suspicions when the deep surveys came back." Arthur kept his expression neutral. "But I filed the proper claims, paid the registration fees—" "Legal ownership isn't in question." Chen stood, pacing before the viewport. "What concerns us is who else knows about your discovery. The Centauri Syndicate has been unusually active lately. Three of their 'merchant' vessels have taken up orbit around Mars. The Europa Cartel has doubled their intelligence assets in Chryse Station. And yesterday, we intercepted communications suggesting the Titan Separatists are planning something... ambitious." "You think they're after my claim." "I think, Mr. Neegan, that your claim has become the focal point of tensions that have been building since humanity spread beyond Earth." Chen returned to his seat. "Which brings me to why you're here. The Science Directorate wants to make you an offer." Arthur waited. "Full partnership. You maintain nominal ownership of the claim, but extraction and research are handled by Directorate teams. In exchange, you receive fifteen percent of all profits, a permanent position on the Azra Research Council, and—most importantly—the protection of the Solar Defense Force." "And if I refuse?" Chen's expression didn't change. "Then you return to Mars, file your extraction permits, and attempt to mine forty-seven million tons of the most valuable and dangerous substance in human space while every major power in the System watches. How long do you think you'd last?" Arthur thought of his small hab-dome, his dozen employees, the simple life he'd built on Mars. "You're asking me to choose sides in a conflict I don't fully understand." "I'm offering you the chance to help prevent that conflict from becoming a war." Chen slid a data pad across the table. "The terms are all there. Take your time reviewing them. But understand this—the moment your discovery became public knowledge, neutrality ceased to be an option." Through the viewport, the stars continued their eternal dance, indifferent to human ambition. Arthur picked up the data pad, knowing that whatever choice he made would ripple across the Solar System like Azra-accelerated decay—inevitable, irreversible, and potentially catastrophic. "How much time do I have to decide?" Chen glanced at his wrist display. "Twelve hours. After that, the Centauri vessels will be in position to begin their approach to Mars." The air filtration system continued its mechanical drone, counting down the seconds like a metallic heartbeat.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a solid understanding of Asimov's style and successfully captures the classic science fiction atmosphere requested. The writing maintains a clean, functional prose style reminiscent of Golden Age sci-fi, with effective world-building that operates on both intimate and galactic scales. The adherence to instructions is excellent - the story follows Arthur aboard a transfer vessel, features dialogue-heavy scenes with vivid descriptions, and establishes the larger political intrigue surrounding the Azra discovery. The author successfully incorporates the retrofuturistic elements (Luna Tech, tachyon manipulation, hab-domes) while maintaining narrative focus. Character actions feel believable and consistent. Arthur responds as an experienced engineer would - cautious, analytical, but not naive about the political implications. Chen is portrayed as a competent bureaucrat with hidden depths, speaking in measured diplomatic language while revealing genuine urgency about the situation. The dialogue serves multiple functions effectively: advancing plot, revealing character, and exposing world-building details. Chen's explanations feel natural rather than expository, and Arthur's responses demonstrate his engineering background and growing understanding of his predicament. The imagery is restrained but effective - the "rust-colored marble" of Mars, the mechanical drone of air filtration systems, and the "metallic heartbeat" create atmosphere without overwhelming the narrative. The descriptions of the station and its military modifications efficiently establish the setting's dual nature. However, the piece has some limitations. While competent, it doesn't reach exceptional heights of creativity or emotional complexity. The political situation, while well-established, follows familiar patterns of corporate/governmental intrigue. Arthur's character, though believable, lacks distinctive personality traits that would make him memorable beyond his circumstances. The pacing is appropriate but somewhat predictable - the revelation builds logically but without significant surprises. The ending, while dramatically appropriate, doesn't offer particular insight or emotional resonance beyond the immediate tension. The prose flows naturally and maintains consistent tone throughout, successfully emulating Asimov's clear, unadorned style without becoming sterile or mechanical. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Emotionally Complex: 12 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 15 Overall Impression: 16

+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 79.8
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Darkroom Revelations** The timer clicks, and I slide the print into the stop bath, watching Elliot's profile emerge in the chemical solution. Even in the darkroom's red glow, I can make out the sharp line of his jaw, the way his oversized Bauhaus shirt drapes over his slight frame. He's bent over the enlarger, adjusting the focus with those careful, precise movements that first caught my attention three months ago. "Dani, can you check if this exposure looks right?" His voice is soft, almost lost in the hum of the ventilation system. I move behind him, close enough to catch the scent of his thrift-store leather jacket mixed with developer chemicals. My usual move would be to place a hand on his shoulder, lean in close, take control of the situation. Instead, I hover, uncertain. "Try stopping down one more," I suggest, keeping my voice neutral. "The highlights are a bit blown out." He nods, makes the adjustment. We've been dancing around each other like this for weeks, ever since Professor Martinez paired us for the final project. Me, the one who usually knows exactly what she wants and takes it. Him, this enigma in platform boots and smudged eyeliner who makes me second-guess everything I thought I knew about my type. "I've been meaning to tell you something," Elliot says suddenly, still focused on the enlarger. "About... me." My heart does this stupid flutter thing. I lean against the counter, trying to look casual. "Yeah?" He turns, and in the red light, his face looks like a Renaissance painting—all shadows and secrets. "I've been questioning. My gender, I mean. Like, what that even means for me." The words hang between us like undeveloped film, waiting to be brought into focus. I realize I'm holding my breath. "I don't know if I'm a boy," he continues, fidgeting with his skull ring. "Sometimes I feel like one, sometimes... not. Sometimes both. Sometimes neither." I want to pull him close, tell him it's okay, that he's perfect however he is. But that's my default—taking charge, being the protector. Maybe that's not what he needs. "Thank you for telling me," I say instead. "That must have taken courage." He laughs, but it's shaky. "You're the first person I've told. Outside of Reddit, anyway." "Why me?" The question slips out before I can stop it. "Because you see people," he says simply. "Really see them. Your portraits—they're not just faces. They're souls." I think about all the times I've watched him in class, cataloguing the way he moves through space like he's apologizing for taking up room, how he lights up when discussing Man Ray or Cindy Sherman, the gentle way he handles the photographs like they're butterfly wings. "I see you," I admit, and it feels like exposing film to light—irreversible, transformative. We return to work, but the air between us has shifted. I'm hyper-aware of every movement, every breath. When he reaches for the tongs at the same time I do, our fingers brush, and neither of us pulls away immediately. "What does this mean for...?" He doesn't finish the question, but I hear it anyway. What does this mean for us? For this thing we've been circling around? "I don't know," I say honestly. It's not like me to not know, to not have a plan. "I've always been attracted to... I mean, I thought I knew what I wanted." I gesture vaguely at myself—my ripped jeans, combat boots, the whole assertive art student aesthetic I've cultivated. "Defined presentations, you know? Clear roles." "And I'm not that," Elliot says. It's not a question. "No," I agree. "You're something else entirely. And that's..." I search for the word. "Exciting? Terrifying? Both?" He smiles then, really smiles, and it transforms his whole face. "I terrify Dani Chen? Should I put that on my resume?" "Shut up," I laugh, shoving his shoulder gently. It's the first time I've touched him intentionally, and even through his shirt, I can feel him tense, then relax. We work in comfortable silence for a while, developing prints side by side. I find myself stealing glances, noticing new things—how he's painted his nails black but let them chip, the way he tucks his hair behind his ear when he's concentrating, the slight curve of his hips in those impossibly tight jeans. "Shit," I mutter suddenly, realizing I've been staring instead of watching what I'm doing. I've lifted the lid of the paper safe while the enlarger was on, exposing the entire stack. "Fuck, I just ruined like fifty sheets." "Hey, it's okay," Elliot says, and suddenly his hand is on my arm, grounding me. "We have more in the supply closet." But I'm not thinking about the paper anymore. I'm thinking about how natural his touch feels, how I want to turn into it, how this person who doesn't know what they are makes me question everything I thought I was. "Elliot," I start, then stop. For once, I don't know how to take control of this moment, and maybe that's the point. Maybe control isn't what either of us needs right now. "Yeah?" He's still touching my arm, thumb tracing small circles. "I don't have answers," I say. "About any of this. But I'd like to... figure it out? With you?" In the red light, his eyes are dark, unreadable. Then he nods, a small movement that feels like a beginning. "Okay," he says. "Okay." We stand there, in the chemical-scented darkness, two people used to being in control of their narratives suddenly finding themselves in uncharted territory. And maybe that's exactly where we need to be.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical competence and largely succeeds in fulfilling the prompt's complex requirements. The narrative effectively captures Dani's perspective as an assertive art student grappling with unexpected feelings and situations that challenge her usual approach to relationships and attraction. The darkroom setting is well-utilized both literally and metaphorically, with the red light creating an intimate, confessional atmosphere that supports the emotional revelations. The author shows good knowledge of photography processes, using technical details naturally without overwhelming the narrative. Character development is nuanced, particularly in portraying Dani's internal conflict between her natural assertiveness and her desire to give Elliot agency in his vulnerable moment. Elliot's gender questioning is handled sensitively and realistically, avoiding both oversimplification and melodrama. The dialogue feels authentic to the characters' ages and situations, with natural hesitations and incomplete thoughts that reflect the awkwardness of the moment. The prose is generally elegant and controlled, with effective sensory details that ground the reader in the darkroom environment. The author successfully balances showing and telling, using physical actions and environmental details to convey emotional states rather than simply stating them. However, there are some areas where the writing could be stronger. Certain phrases lean toward the overwrought ("souls," "butterfly wings"), and some metaphors feel slightly forced (the undeveloped film comparison). The pacing occasionally slows when the internal monologue becomes too explanatory. The ending, while appropriate, feels somewhat predictable in its tentative hopefulness. The emotional complexity is well-handled, particularly Dani's confusion about her own identity and attraction patterns. The piece avoids easy answers while still providing a satisfying sense of forward movement in the relationship. + Contemporary romance / drama: Stolen Moments in Roma — Score: 83.8
Prompt:
Contemporary Romance / Drama: Stolen Moments in Roma. Write a series of interconnected short scenes and dialogues that span a single day in modern-day Rome, in narrative form. This will form the beginning of a larger story. Alessandra, a fiery, independent Roman woman who works as a motorcycle courier, nearly collides with Ben, a somewhat clumsy, overly polite British academic on sabbatical, who is engrossed in a guidebook near the Trevi Fountain. The initial encounter is a clash of cultures and personalities – Alessandra is all sharp wit and rapid-fire Italian, while Ben is flustered apologies and awkward attempts at speaking the language. However, as they sort out the near-miss, a spark of unexpected humor and connection emerges. Alessandra, it quickly becomes clear, is a whirlwind of energy and mischief. She's a self-admitted troublemaker with a secret penchant for kleptomania, a detail she doesn't explicitly reveal, but which manifests in subtle, daring acts throughout the day. She impulsively decides to take Ben on a wild, unsanctioned tour of Rome, dragging him in some specific instances far outside his comfort zone. Ben, despite his initial apprehension and inherent politeness, finds himself utterly captivated by Alessandra's unapologetic spirit and zest for life. He is drawn into her orbit, a willing participant in increasingly outlandish situations, a quiet rebellion brewing beneath his scholarly exterior. The narrative should primarily consist of short, vivid scenes and dialogue exchanges, revealing their contrasting personalities and backgrounds, and the growing, ambiguous tension between them. Ben is captivated by Alessandra's energy and the thrill of breaking free from his routine. There's a clear sense that Alessandra is toying with Ben, pushing his boundaries and enjoying his reactions, but her own feelings remain somewhat enigmatic. The day should be a rollercoaster of exhilarating, slightly dangerous, and humorous moments, leaving Ben completely disoriented but utterly smitten. Explore the underlying tension & chemistry between them, culminating in some payoff as they part ways (or not -- your choice). Include this detail: Write in narrative prose, using immersive storytelling. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Stolen Moments in Roma** The Vespa's engine cut through the morning chaos near the Trevi Fountain like a hot knife through gelato. Alessandra Rossi leaned into the turn, her leather jacket rippling in the Roman breeze, when a figure stumbled backward directly into her path. "Merda!" She yanked the handlebars hard left, tires squealing against ancient cobblestones. The motorcycle lurched to a stop mere inches from a man clutching a guidebook like a life preserver. "Oh God, I'm terribly sorry! Mi dispiace! No, wait—mi scusi!" The British accent mangled the Italian as badly as his feet had mangled her route. Alessandra killed the engine and flipped up her helmet visor, dark eyes blazing. "Che cazzo fai? You trying to get yourself killed, tourist?" "I was just—the guidebook said the best angle for photographs—" Ben fumbled with his book, nearly dropping it. His wire-rimmed glasses sat askew on his nose. "The best angle is not under my wheels." She dismounted in one fluid motion, noting how he stepped back nervously. The Vespa bore its battle scars proudly—a rainbow scratch along the left side from that incident with the ambassador's gate last month. "Right. Yes. Of course." He straightened his glasses, then extended a hand. "Ben Hartley. I really am dreadfully sorry." She ignored the hand, circling him like a cat evaluating prey. "British. Professor type. First time in Roma?" "How did you—" "The shoes." She pointed at his sensible walking loafers. "The guidebook. The way you apologize for breathing." A grin tugged at her lips. "How long you here?" "Six months. Sabbatical. I'm researching—" "Boring." She swung back onto the Vespa. "You want to see Roma? Real Roma?" Ben blinked. "I'm sorry?" "Get on." She patted the seat behind her. "I couldn't possibly—" "You almost died under my Vespa. Least I can do is show you why it would have been worth it." Her smile turned wicked. "Unless you prefer your little book?" --- Twenty minutes later, Ben's knuckles were white as he gripped Alessandra's waist, his academic vocabulary reduced to occasional whimpers as she wove through traffic like a demented seamstress. "The Pantheon is that way!" he shouted over the engine. "Exactly!" She accelerated in the opposite direction. They screeched to a halt in Trastevere, where laundry fluttered between buildings like prayer flags. Alessandra hopped off, leaving Ben to peel himself from the seat. "Breakfast," she announced, dragging him toward a tiny café. "It's nearly noon—" "In Roma, breakfast is when you eat it." She ordered for both of them in rapid-fire Italian, the barista laughing at something she said. Ben caught maybe every tenth word. "What did you tell him?" "That you're my British prisoner and I'm showing you how civilization works." She slid a cornetto across to him, then deftly palmed the sugar dispenser from the next table into her jacket pocket. "Eat. You'll need energy." "For what?" Her only answer was that dangerous smile. --- The afternoon dissolved into a blur of narrow alleys and hidden courtyards. Alessandra moved through Rome like water, always finding the gaps, the secret passages. She showed him a garden where Caesar once walked ("Probably," she admitted), a wall where Keats had carved his initials ("Maybe"), and a fountain where wishes came true ("Definitely not, but tourists love that shit"). At a street market, she haggled ferociously over leather gloves while Ben watched, mesmerized by her hands as they gestured and argued. He didn't notice when those same hands liberated a silk scarf from a neighboring stall, tucking it away with practiced ease. "You speak beautifully," he said as they walked away, him carrying the gloves she'd insisted on buying for him. "My English? Is terrible." "Your Italian. It's like... music. Violent music, but still." She stopped walking, studying him. "You're not what I expected, Professor." "What did you expect?" "Boring. Scared. Running away by now." She reached up and straightened his glasses, her fingers lingering a moment too long. "Maybe you have some Roman in you after all." --- As the sun painted the city gold, they sat on a wall overlooking the Forum, sharing a bottle of wine she'd produced from her seemingly bottomless jacket. "I should probably know your last name," Ben said, "considering I've spent the day clinging to you on a death machine." "Rossi. Like the motorcycle racer." She took a swig from the bottle. "You know what your problem is, Ben Hartley?" "I have a feeling you're about to tell me." "You think too much. All day, I watch you thinking. Analyzing. Filing everything away in that professor brain." She tapped his temple. "You're in Roma. You should be feeling." "I am feeling. Predominantly terror, but also..." He paused, searching for words. "Also?" "Alive." The admission seemed to surprise him. "More alive than I've felt in years." She kissed him then, swift and fierce, tasting of wine and mischief. When she pulled away, his glasses were fogged and his expression dazed. "See? Feeling." She hopped down from the wall. "Come. One more stop." --- The motorcycle climbed the Janiculum Hill as twilight settled over the city. At the top, Roma spread below them like a jewelry box spilled across velvet. "This is my secret place," Alessandra said, killing the engine. "I come here when the city gets too small." "It's incredible." Ben dismounted, still unsteady. "Today has been... I don't have words." "Good. Words are overrated." She pulled the stolen scarf from her pocket, wrapping it around his neck. "A souvenir." "Alessandra, I can't—" "Shut up." But she said it gently. "You go back to your books tomorrow, yes? Your research, your routine?" "I... suppose so." "Then tonight, you don't think. Tonight, you're Roman." She moved closer, the city lights reflected in her dark eyes. "Unless you have somewhere else to be?" Ben looked at this impossible woman who'd crashed into his day like a beautiful disaster. His guidebook was long forgotten, probably still lying on some ancient street. His carefully planned itinerary was in ruins. He'd never been happier. "Nowhere else," he said, and pulled her close as Roma glittered below, eternal and patient, keeping their secrets safe.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates the requested interconnected scenes spanning a single day in Rome, establishing clear character contrasts between the fiery Italian motorcycle courier Alessandra and the bumbling British academic Ben. The cultural clash is well-rendered through dialogue and behavior patterns. The characterization is particularly effective. Alessandra emerges as a vivid, three-dimensional character whose kleptomania is subtly woven throughout (the sugar dispenser, the silk scarf) without being heavy-handed. Her dialogue crackles with authentic voice - sharp, direct, and commanding. Ben's transformation from nervous academic to willing participant feels organic and well-paced across the day's progression. The prose quality is consistently strong, with evocative imagery that brings Rome to life without becoming overwrought. Phrases like "The Vespa's engine cut through the morning chaos near the Trevi Fountain like a hot knife through gelato" establish tone and setting efficiently. The author demonstrates restraint, avoiding purple prose while maintaining descriptive richness. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes - advancing plot, revealing character, and building romantic tension. The cultural and linguistic barriers are handled with humor rather than stereotype. Alessandra's mix of Italian and English feels authentic, and Ben's awkward attempts at the language ring true. The romantic development is well-earned, building through shared experiences rather than instant attraction. The kiss scene feels like a natural culmination of growing chemistry rather than a forced romantic beat. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - hopeful but not unrealistically transformative for a single day's encounter. The structure works well, with clear scene breaks that maintain momentum while allowing character development. The pacing accelerates appropriately as Ben becomes more comfortable with Alessandra's world. The author successfully captures the "whirlwind day" feeling while maintaining narrative coherence. Minor weaknesses include some slightly predictable romantic beats and a few moments where Ben's transformation could have been more gradually developed. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness of the piece. + Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 83.0
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The tentacles drooping from the stranger's head twitch as I slide the double Vega whisky across the bar's worn synth-wood surface. They're a deep purple, almost black at the tips, and I can't help but notice how they curl away from the amber liquid like they're trying to escape. "Rough day?" I ask, polishing a glass with practiced movements. The Outpost hums with its usual evening crowd—a pair of helium miners arguing over a game of quantum dice in the corner, and old Marta crooning some Earthen ballad to her fourth beer. The stranger's main eyes—all four of them—flick up to meet mine. "You could say that." They down half the whisky in one go. "You're Nettle?" I pause mid-polish. "Nets to my friends. We friends?" "Could be." They extend a humanoid hand, though I notice the webbing between their fingers. "Kass. Short for... well, it doesn't translate. I heard you're the one to talk to about finding people who don't want to be found." My chest tightens, but I keep my expression neutral. That's not a reputation I've cultivated. In fact, it's exactly the kind of reputation I've been trying to avoid. "You heard wrong. I pour drinks and occasionally mediate disputes over mining claims. That's it." "Right." Kass's tentacles writhe, a sure sign of agitation in their species. "Look, I'm not Authority. I'm not a bounty hunter. I just need to find someone, and I'm willing to pay." Behind us, glass shatters against the floor. I glance over to see Tomás, one of our regulars, staring at his empty hand in confusion while his drinking buddy laughs. "Gravity's different on Station Seven!" his friend chortles. "Told you to use both hands!" I grab the dustpan from under the bar. "Be right back. Don't go anywhere." As I weave through the tables, my mind races. How does this Kass know about my past? I've been careful. Three years at the Outpost without a single slip-up. Well, except for that incident with the Vorlan smugglers, but I'd made sure there were no witnesses to my... particular skills. "Nets, honey, can you tell Rikko that I was here first?" Marta waves me over, her silver hair catching the neon from the beer signs. "He's trying to claim my usual booth for his chess tournament." "Marta, you know Tuesday is chess night," I say, sweeping up the glass shards. "Besides, you hate that booth. Too close to the recycling units." She grins, caught. "Worth a shot. Can't blame an old woman for trying to squeeze a free drink out of management." "Rikko doesn't even work here." "Details, details." I dump the glass in the recycler and head back to the bar, where Kass is nursing the rest of their whisky. Their tentacles have calmed somewhat, now swaying gently like kelp in a cosmic current. "So," I say, resuming my glass polishing, "who told you I find people?" "Does it matter?" "It does to me." Kass sighs, a sound like wind through a pipe organ. "Vex Mulligan. Said you helped him track down his daughter last year." I nearly drop the glass. Vex's daughter hadn't been missing—she'd been hiding from him, and for good reason. I'd helped her disappear properly, not find her. Which means either Vex figured out what I really did, or... "Vex is dead," I say carefully. "Mining accident two months ago." "I know." Kass leans forward, tentacles spreading across the bar like spilled wine. "But before he died, he sent a message to my employer. Said Nettle at the Last Outpost could find anyone in the Outer Rim. Even people who'd been professionally disappeared." My mouth goes dry. I pour myself a shot of the cheap stuff, knock it back. "Your employer?" "That's where it gets complicated." Kass glances around, then produces a small holo-projector. The image that springs up makes my blood run cold. It's me. Not Nets the bartender, but the me from before. The me I've spent three years burying. "Ariadne Corso," Kass says quietly. "Former Intelligence Division. Specialized in witness protection and identity reassignment. Went rogue three years ago after—" "Stop." My voice comes out harder than intended. Several patrons glance our way. I force a smile, wave them off. "That person doesn't exist anymore." "My employer thinks differently." Kass deactivates the holo. "They need someone found. Someone only you can find, because you're the one who hid them." "Even if I was this person—which I'm not—what makes you think I'd help?" Kass's four eyes blink in sequence, a gesture I recognize as their species' equivalent of a shrug. "Because if you don't, my employer will make sure everyone in the Outer Rim knows exactly who's been pouring their drinks for the past three years. Including the people you've been hiding from." The Outpost suddenly feels too small, too exposed. Through the viewports, asteroids drift by in their eternal dance, and I wish I could join them—just float away into the black. "Who?" I ask finally. "Who does your employer want found?" Kass's tentacles curl with what might be satisfaction. Or maybe dread. "Jenna Park. Ring any bells?" The glass I'm polishing slips from my hands, shattering against the floor in an echo of Tomás's accident. But this time, nobody laughs. Jenna Park. The one person I swore I'd never give up. The one person whose location could destroy everything.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt requirements, delivering a cozy sci-fi setting with approximately 60% dialogue in first-person present tense. The author establishes The Last Outpost as a lived-in space with diverse alien patrons and creates compelling conflict through Kass's arrival and knowledge of Nets's hidden past. The characterization is particularly effective. Nets feels authentic as someone trying to escape her past while building a new life - her bartending skills, careful neutrality, and protective instincts all ring true. The supporting characters like Marta and Tomás add texture to the world without feeling extraneous. Kass works well as a mysterious antagonist whose tentacled alien physiology is woven naturally into their emotional expressions. The dialogue flows naturally and serves multiple purposes - advancing plot, revealing character, and building atmosphere. Conversations feel organic, from Marta's playful manipulation attempts to the increasingly tense exchange between Nets and Kass. The author skillfully uses interruptions and bar business to create realistic pacing and allow Nets thinking time. The world-building strikes an appropriate balance for cozy sci-fi, incorporating alien species, asteroid mining, and futuristic technology without overwhelming the intimate bar setting. Details like "synth-wood," "quantum dice," and species-specific behaviors feel integrated rather than forced. The central conflict builds effectively from Nets's initial wariness to the revelation of her true identity as Ariadne Corso, culminating in the bombshell request to find Jenna Park. The pacing allows tension to escalate naturally while maintaining the cozy atmosphere until the final revelation. Some minor weaknesses include occasional exposition that could be more subtle and a few descriptive phrases that border on overwrought. The prose generally maintains elegance but has moments where similes feel slightly forced ("tentacles spreading across the bar like spilled wine"). The ending delivers genuine surprise and emotional impact, with the glass-breaking parallel providing nice structural symmetry. The piece successfully establishes conflict without resolving it, leaving readers invested in the outcome. + Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 82.8
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The saxophone wails like it knows my whole damn story. I'm standing on Royal Street with exactly thirty-seven dollars in my pocket, a dead phone, and sneakers that smell like the trucker's cigarettes from last night. The musician—old Black guy with a fedora—catches my eye and winks. I look away fast. Can't afford to put money in his case, can't afford the attention either. New Orleans hits different than I expected. It's October but sweat's already pooling under my binder, which I've been wearing for way too long. The air tastes like sugar and garbage and something else—freedom, maybe? Or just different kinds of danger than the ones I left behind. *Stop being dramatic, Taylor. You wanted this.* A tour group pushes past, all matching t-shirts and sunburns, and I let myself get swept along for half a block before peeling off. The French Quarter's a maze of balconies dripping with plants and rust. Everyone seems to know where they're going except me. "You lost, baby?" The voice belongs to a kid about my age, maybe younger, sitting cross-legged against a bright blue wall. They've got bleached hair with dark roots, ripped fishnets under shorts, and the kind of eyeliner I could never pull off back home. There's a cardboard sign next to them but it's flipped backward. "No," I say automatically. They laugh. "Yeah, okay. You got that fresh-off-the-bus look. Trust me, I know it." I should keep walking. Mom always said I trusted too easy, made friends with anyone who showed me attention. *Look where that got you*, whispers the part of my brain that sounds like Dad after two beers. But this kid's smile seems real, and I'm so fucking tired. "Maybe a little lost," I admit. "I'm Ash. They/them." They pat the ground next to them. "Cop check?" "What?" "You a cop? You'd have to tell me." "I'm sixteen." "That's not an answer." But they're grinning wider now. "Kidding. Mostly. You hungry?" My stomach answers before I can, loud enough that the saxophone player probably heard it. Ash flips their sign around. It says "Anything helps. God bless" in purple sharpie. "Good morning so far," they say, counting bills. "Split a po'boy with me? You look like you need it." Pride wars with hunger. Hunger wins. We walk to a place Ash knows, dodging puddles that never seem to dry even though it hasn't rained. They order for both of us in an accent that's not quite Southern, not quite anything. "So," Ash says, mouth full of fried shrimp. "What's your damage?" "My what?" "Your story. Nobody ends up sleeping rough in the Quarter without one." *Sleeping rough*. Is that what I'm doing now? "I'm not—I have money." "Sure, cher. How much?" I don't answer. Ash shrugs, tears off half their sandwich and slides it over. "I'll go first then. Ran away from my group home in Baton Rouge six months ago. They kept trying to fix me." They make air quotes around 'fix.' "Like being nonbinary is something you can pray away. You know how it is." My chest tightens. I do know. God, I know. "Yeah," I manage. Ash studies me closer. "Pronouns?" The question I've been dreading and hoping for. Back home, I'd practiced in the mirror. *Hi, I'm Taylor, he/him*. But the words that came out of my mouth two nights ago were different. Messier. I'd said "I'm trans" and Mom's face had just... shut down. Like someone pulled the plug. "He/him," I whisper. Then louder: "He/him." "Cool. You got a place to crash tonight, Taylor?" I shake my head. The thirty-seven dollars feels smaller by the minute. "There's a squat I know. Mostly queer kids. It's not fancy but it's safer than the streets." Ash pauses. "Relatively." *Don't trust strangers. Don't follow people you just met. Don't—* "There's rules though," Ash continues. "No hard drugs. No bringing cops around. No starting shit with the other kids. And everybody contributes something—money, food, whatever. It's not a free ride." "I can contribute." "Yeah? What can you do?" What can I do? I can rebuild a carburetor, throw a perfect spiral, quote entire episodes of The Office. None of that feels useful here. "I'll figure it out." Ash laughs again, but not mean. "That's what we all said." The saxophone follows us through the Quarter as Ash leads me deeper into the city. They point out landmarks—where to get free water, which coffee shops let you use the bathroom, which corners to avoid after dark. My head spins trying to remember it all. "You're scared," Ash observes. "That's smart. Stay scared, stay alive. But also—" They stop walking, turn to face me. "This city will eat you up if you let it. Or it can be home. Depends on what you're willing to become." *What I'm willing to become*. Like I have any idea what that is. Two days ago I was someone's daughter. Now I'm just another runaway with a gender identity crisis and a death wish, according to Dad's last text before I blocked his number. But maybe that's not all I am. Maybe that's not all I have to be. "Okay," I tell Ash. "Show me this squat." They smile like I passed some kind of test. The saxophone fades as we turn onto a quieter street, and I follow this stranger who uses my pronouns into whatever comes next. *You wanted this*, I remind myself. And for the first time in 48 hours, I think maybe I actually did.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully capturing Taylor's voice as a transgender teen navigating their first day in New Orleans after running away. The writing maintains a consistent first-person perspective with mixed tense usage as requested, and incorporates both internal monologue and dialogue effectively. The characterization is particularly strong. Taylor feels authentic as a frightened but determined 16-year-old, with believable reactions to their new environment. The internal conflict between desperation and pride, fear and hope, creates emotional complexity. Ash serves as an effective foil - experienced but not cynical, helpful but with clear boundaries. Their introduction of pronouns feels natural and meaningful within the context. The prose strikes a good balance between accessibility and literary quality. Sensory details like "The air tastes like sugar and garbage and something else" and "sneakers that smell like the trucker's cigarettes" ground the reader in the setting without becoming overwrought. The saxophone motif provides effective atmospheric continuity. Dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, particularly Ash's street-smart vernacular and Taylor's more hesitant responses. The conversation flows organically while revealing character and advancing plot. The pronoun exchange is handled with particular skill - neither preachy nor overly dramatic. The emotional arc is well-calibrated. Taylor's journey from complete disorientation to tentative hope feels earned rather than rushed. The ending strikes an appropriate note of cautious optimism without minimizing the real dangers ahead. Some minor weaknesses include occasional reliance on familiar runaway narrative beats and a few moments where the internal monologue becomes slightly heavy-handed. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. The writing demonstrates technical competence with varied sentence structure and natural flow. The piece maintains reader engagement throughout, successfully balancing exposition with action and character development within the word count constraints. + Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 82.0
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**My Apocalypse Buddy** The morning sun filtered through the makeshift curtain of duct tape and cereal boxes that Midas had fashioned into what he generously called a "window treatment." It cast prison-bar shadows across the floor of their shelter—a former Hot Dog on a Stick that had somehow survived the Great Unpleasantness mostly intact, though the giant lemonade pitcher sign now hung at a decidedly jaunty angle. "Right," said Midas, addressing the animatronic koala perched on the counter. "Today's the day we check out that graffiti." Chewy's glassy eyes stared back with the infinite patience that only the battery-powered possess. His internal mechanisms whirred softly, a sound Midas had come to find oddly comforting in the profound silence of the After. "EUCALYPTUS IS NUTRITIOUS," the koala announced in its tinny voice, one of twelve pre-programmed phrases that had survived whatever electronic apocalypse had taken down the internet, smartphones, and Midas's chance of ever finishing that online philosophy degree. "Yes, thank you for that insight," Midas muttered, checking his supplies. Three cans of beans (expiration date: optimistic), a half-empty bottle of water (taste: questionable), and a golf club he'd named Gerald. "The graffiti said 'Paradise Waits Where the Iron Bird Fell.' That's got to mean something, right?" The koala's head swiveled ninety degrees with a mechanical click. "G'DAY MATE!" Midas had found Chewy in the ruins of a toy store six months ago. The thing's solar panel belly kept it running during daylight hours, making it the closest thing to a sustainable companion in this brave new world. The fact that it occasionally offered relevant responses to his questions was either miraculous or a sign that Midas had finally cracked. He preferred not to dwell on which. They set out into the wasteland that had once been suburban sprawl. Nature, it turned out, was remarkably quick to reclaim its territory when humans stopped mowing things. Vines strangled stop signs, and what Midas was pretty sure had been a Starbucks was now home to a family of raccoons who'd learned to operate the manual espresso machine. "Iron bird," Midas mused, stepping over a crack in the pavement that had become a small canyon. "Plane crash, maybe?" "KOALAS SLEEP TWENTY HOURS A DAY," Chewy informed him from his position in Midas's backpack, fuzzy head poking out like the world's least helpful periscope. Three hours of walking brought them to what had indeed been a plane crash—a small Cessna wrapped around an oak tree like a metallic bow. The graffiti artist had been there too: "GETTING WARMER" in the same angular script, with an arrow pointing toward a cluster of buildings in the distance. "This feels like a trap," Midas said. "CRIKEY!" agreed Chewy. They pressed on anyway, because what else was there to do? Television was gone, and Midas had already read every book in a three-mile radius, including a disturbingly comprehensive collection of vampire romance novels he'd found in a bunker. The buildings turned out to be an abandoned research facility, its parking lot sprouting with dandelions that had achieved an almost aggressive cheerfulness. Another message adorned the main entrance: "CONGRATULATIONS! YOU'VE WON NOTHING!" "Fantastic," Midas sighed. "A nihilist with spray paint." Inside, the facility was a maze of corridors that all looked identical, like someone had tried to build an IKEA but forgot the meatballs and Swedish efficiency. Midas navigated by following the graffiti, each message more cryptic than the last: "LEFT IS RIGHT," "RIGHT IS WRONG," "STRAIGHT IS CURVED IN HIGHER DIMENSIONS." "I'M ENDANGERED!" Chewy announced, which seemed appropriate. They finally reached a heavy door marked "DEFINITELY NOT PARADISE." Inside was... a supply closet. A well-stocked supply closet, admittedly, with shelves of canned goods, bottled water, and—Midas's heart skipped—toilet paper. "It's beautiful," he whispered. That's when the door slammed shut behind them. "Oh, come on!" Midas yanked at the handle. Locked. Of course. A final message glowed faintly on the interior wall, written in what appeared to be glow-in-the-dark paint: "PARADISE REQUIRES SACRIFICE. WAIT THREE DAYS OR SOLVE THE RIDDLE." Under this was what looked like advanced calculus mixed with ancient Sumerian. "EUCALYPTUS IS NUTRITIOUS," Chewy offered. "Not helping, mate." Midas spent the next six hours trying everything: attempted mathematics, interpretive dance, reasoning with the door, and finally just hitting things with Gerald the golf club. It was this last approach that revealed the emergency exit release, cunningly hidden behind a sign that said "NOT AN EMERGENCY EXIT RELEASE." They emerged into the evening air, Midas's backpack now heavy with liberated supplies. The journey home was complicated by the discovery that the raccoons had formed a toll bridge operation, demanding payment in shiny objects. Midas sacrificed three buttons and a can opener before Chewy's sudden cry of "DANGER! DANGER!" scared them into the underbrush. "You know," Midas said as they finally glimpsed the tilted lemonade pitcher in the distance, "I'm starting to think that graffiti artist might just be an asshole." "KOALAS ARE NOT BEARS," Chewy noted philosophically. "No," Midas agreed, patting the koala's synthetic fur head. "No, they're not." As they settled in for the night, Midas reflected that perhaps paradise wasn't a place at all. Maybe it was just having a companion who never judged your life choices, even if their conversation skills were limited to marsupial facts. Outside, somewhere in the dark, spray paint hissed against concrete.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece successfully captures the wry, dry humor characteristic of Terry Pratchett while establishing a compelling post-apocalyptic setting. The author demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt, delivering exactly what was requested: a story beginning with deliberation, featuring a mission that goes sideways, and ending with the protagonists returning home worse for wear. The characterization is particularly effective. Midas emerges as a believable everyman protagonist whose philosophical musings and resigned acceptance of absurd circumstances feel authentic to both the Pratchett style and post-apocalyptic genre. The animatronic koala Chewy is brilliantly conceived - limited to twelve pre-programmed phrases yet somehow managing to be both comic relief and genuine companion. The dynamic between them feels natural and earned. The voice remains consistently dry and observational throughout, with excellent Pratchett-esque touches like "the Great Unpleasantness," the parenthetical asides, and the matter-of-fact treatment of absurd situations. The prose flows well with good pacing and natural sentence structure. The world-building is economical but vivid - the Hot Dog on a Stick shelter, raccoons operating espresso machines, and nature reclaiming civilization paint a clear picture without over-explanation. The imagery strikes the right balance between descriptive and functional. The plot structure works well, with the graffiti mystery providing clear motivation and the supply closet trap delivering the promised complications. The ending avoids false positivity while finding genuine warmth in the companionship theme. However, the piece has some weaknesses. The dialogue, while functional, lacks the sparkling wit that elevates Pratchett's best work. Some descriptions veer toward the pedestrian, and certain plot elements (the locked door, the emergency exit) feel somewhat mechanical. The emotional complexity, while present, could be deeper. The story is engaging but not particularly surprising in its developments. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 2 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 83.0
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The bell above the door jangled frantically as someone burst through, bringing with them a gust of Welsh autumn air and the distant sound of shouting voices. "Sorry, we're actually clos—" "Please." The man pressed his back against the door, chest heaving. "Just five minutes." Meredith looked up from her inventory clipboard, taking in the designer sunglasses, artfully tousled dark hair, and leather jacket that probably cost more than her monthly rent. Through the window, she spotted a cluster of photographers jogging past, cameras at the ready. "Paparazzi?" She returned to her counting. "Third shelf behind Poetry. They never check there." "You're an angel." He flashed a smile that had graced a thousand magazine covers. "I'm Oliver, by the way. Oliver Rhys." "I know who you are." Meredith made another tick on her list. "Seventeen... eighteen..." "And you are?" "Counting." Oliver laughed—that famous throaty chuckle that had charmed talk show hosts across three continents. "I meant your name." "Twenty-two... Meredith." "Meredith." He rolled it around like wine on his tongue. "That's lovely. Very Welsh." "Very observant. We are in Wales." He sidled closer, leaning against her counter. "You know, most people get a bit... flustered when I walk in." "Twenty-eight... twenty-nine... Most people haven't had Benedict Cumberbatch vomit on their shoes." Oliver's eyebrows shot up. "I'm sorry, what?" "Book signing. Two years ago. Terrible flu, poor man. Now celebrities just seem very... human." She glanced at him. "Thirty-one. You've made me lose count." "My deepest apologies." He didn't look sorry at all. "Though I notice you remember the exact date Benedict Cumberbatch visited." "Hard to forget replacing one's favorite boots." "And yet you're counting inventory when Oliver Rhys is in your shop." "Oliver Rhys interrupted my closing routine." She started over at the next shelf. "One... two..." "You really don't care that I'm here, do you?" Something shifted in his voice—the polished charm cracking slightly. "Should I? Three... four..." "I was in Gladiator Brigade." "Congratulations." "And The Manhattan Principle." "Five... six... Never saw it." "It grossed three hundred million worldwide!" "Seven... Did it? How nice for you." Oliver fell silent. Meredith could feel him watching her as she moved along the shelf. When she glanced back, he'd removed his sunglasses, revealing those impossibly green eyes that had launched a thousand fan sites. "You're really not impressed at all." "Should I swoon? Fetch you a coffee? Ask for a selfie?" She turned to face him fully. "I run a bookshop, Mr. Rhys. I'm surrounded by thousands of stories every day. Real magic. Forgive me if I don't genuflect because you played dress-up for money." His jaw dropped. Then, unexpectedly, he laughed—not the practiced chuckle, but something genuine and delighted. "God, you're magnificent. No one's spoken to me like that in years." "Ah. So you're one of those." "Those?" "Men who mistake disinterest for flirtation." "Are you disinterested?" "I'm trying to close my shop." "That wasn't an answer." Meredith felt heat creeping up her neck. His eyes really were unnaturally green. "Where's your entourage? Surely Oliver Rhys doesn't wander Hay-on-Wye alone." "Gave them the slip." He picked up a bookmark, fidgeting with it. "Sometimes I just need to... not be him. You know?" "Must be terrible, all that adoration." "You'd be surprised." The bravado dimmed again. "Last month, I checked into a hotel under a fake name. Told them I was an accountant. The desk clerk barely looked at me. I sat in that room for three hours just... enjoying being nobody." He set down the bookmark. "Pathetic, right?" "A little." At his wince, she softened. "But human." "There's that word again." He studied her. "What about you? What's Meredith-who-counts-books afraid of?" "Bold assumption." "Everyone's afraid of something." She hesitated, then surprised herself by answering. "Airports." "Fear of flying?" "Fear of possibility." The words tumbled out unbidden. "All those departure boards, all those places I could go. Instead, I'm here, counting inventory in a shop I inherited, in a town I've never left, because choosing to stay nowhere feels safer than choosing wrong." The confession hung between them. Oliver's expression had gone thoughtful, the performative charm completely absent now. "That's the most honest thing anyone's said to me in months." "Yes, well." Meredith turned back to her shelves, mortified. "Twelve... thirteen..." "I could help." "Count inventory?" "See the world. I travel constantly. You could—" "What? Tag along? Be your bookish Welsh accessory?" "That's not what I meant." "Isn't it?" She faced him again. "You don't even know me." "I know you're the first person in years who hasn't asked for a photo. Who doesn't laugh at my jokes when they aren't funny. Who just told me something real." He stepped closer. "I know your hair escapes from that ponytail when you're concentrating. That you bite your lip when you're trying not to smile." "I don't—" But she was doing it right then. "And I know the paparazzi left ten minutes ago, but I'm still here." Meredith's heart hammered against her ribs. "Your ego needed feeding. I was a challenge." "Was?" "You're very transparent, Mr. Rhys." "Am I?" He reached past her for a book, his arm brushing hers. "Then what am I thinking now?" She could smell his cologne—something expensive and woody that made her think of forests after rain. "That you've played this scene before. Different bookshops, different women." "Wrong." His voice dropped. "I'm thinking I want to know why someone who dreams of departure boards stays. What books you read. Whether you drink coffee or tea. If you'll have dinner with me—not Oliver Rhys, just Oliver." "I don't date customers." "I haven't bought anything." "An oversight I can remedy." She pulled a book from the shelf. "Here. Welsh poetry. You could use some culture." He grinned. "Will you sign it?" "I didn't write it." "Sign it anyway. 'To Oliver, who interrupted my counting.'" Despite herself, Meredith laughed. "You're ridiculous." "Ridiculously charming?" "Just ridiculous." But she was already reaching for a pen.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates the requested Welsh bookstore setting and establishes the paparazzi-escape scenario that brings the characters together. The dialogue-heavy approach works well, with each character maintaining distinct voices throughout - Oliver's performative charm gradually giving way to vulnerability, and Meredith's dry, unimpressed wit masking her growing attraction. The characterization is particularly effective. Oliver is convincingly portrayed as someone whose charm is both genuine and calculated, with moments of real vulnerability breaking through his practiced persona. His admission about checking into hotels under fake names feels authentic and adds depth beyond the typical "famous person problems" trope. Meredith's character is well-developed through her responses and the revelation about her fear of airports/possibility, which serves as an effective parallel to Oliver's fear of being nobody. The chemistry between the characters builds naturally through their verbal sparring. The author skillfully shows Meredith's growing attraction through small details - her losing count, biting her lip, the way she softens when Oliver shows vulnerability. The power dynamic shifts believably as Oliver realizes his usual charm isn't working and becomes more genuine. The prose is clean and efficient, with good pacing and natural dialogue flow. The Welsh setting is established without heavy-handedness, and details like the Benedict Cumberbatch anecdote add humor while reinforcing Meredith's character. The writing avoids purple prose while still creating atmosphere. However, the piece has some minor weaknesses. The ending feels slightly rushed - the transition from Meredith's wariness to her willingness to sign the book could use more development. Some of Oliver's more vulnerable moments, while well-written, border on being too convenient for generating sympathy. The "fear of airports" confession, while thematically relevant, feels somewhat forced in its delivery. The piece succeeds in creating genuine romantic tension without relying on clichés, and both characters feel like real people rather than romance novel archetypes. The dialogue crackles with wit and subtext, and the emotional complexity feels earned rather than manufactured. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 5 Unearned Transformations: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 83.7
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The door groaned open on rust-eaten hinges, revealing a chamber lit by the sickly green glow of phosphorescent lamps. Aya's breath caught in her throat. There, seated upon a throne cobbled from whale vertebrae and brass piping, sat Madame Volkov—very much not rotting in Queensgate Prison. "Well, well." The guild leader's voice dripped like oil through gears. "The little masked sparrow finally comes to roost." Six figures emerged from the shadows, their faces obscured by leather masks fitted with brass breathing apparatus. The click of hammers being drawn back echoed through the vaulted space. Katra's hand twitched toward her blade, but Simeon caught her wrist. "Steady on," he muttered through tobacco-stained teeth. Madame Volkov rose, her mechanical leg hissing with each step. The device was a marvel of brass and steel, articulated with tiny pistons that wheezed in the damp air. "Did you truly believe the Constabulary could hold me? That their little iron cages could contain what I've become?" Aya's mind raced. *The raid—a ruse. She wanted to see who would make a play for power.* The weight of her porcelain mask suddenly felt suffocating. "Nothing to say, little sparrow?" Volkov circled them like a shark scenting blood in the water. "You, who whispered such dark promises in the shadows. Who made the rats dance and the dead things writhe." A foghorn bellowed across the harbor—once, twice, thrice. The sound reverberated through the stone, making the phosphorescent lamps flicker. "Strip her mask," Volkov commanded. Two bodyguards seized Aya before she could summon the immateria. Rough hands tore away the porcelain facade, revealing her pale aristocratic features. Katra swore colorfully. "God's bleeding wounds," breathed one of the bodyguards. "It's the Blackwood girl." Volkov's scarred face split into a grin that belonged on a corpse. "Lady Aya Blackwood. Daughter of the late Viscountess—the witch they stretched at Tyburn Square." She savored each word like aged whiskey. "How delicious." *Think. Think.* Aya's mother had taught her that panic was the enemy of survival. Even as the bodyguards forced her to her knees on the wet stone, she kept her voice level. "If you know who I am, then you know my value." "Value?" Volkov laughed, a sound like grinding gears. "Your value to me was as a talented lieutenant. Your value to your dear papa, however..." She gestured, and her men seized Katra and Simeon as well, disarming them with practiced efficiency. "The Duke would pay handsomely for his wayward daughter's return," Aya pressed, though the words tasted like ash. "More than the artifact could ever bring you." "The artifact?" Volkov's mechanical leg hissed as she crouched before Aya. "Child, you understand so little. The Scythan relic isn't about mere coin." Behind Volkov, something writhed in a brass containment vessel—something that made Aya's gift for immateria magick recoil in recognition. The thing pulsed with a light that hurt to perceive directly. "Thirty years I've led this guild," Volkov continued. "Thirty years of keeping the old ways alive while the world above us churns toward its mechanical future. Steam and steel, brass and oil—yes, these things have their place. But there are older powers. Powers that swim in the deep places between stars." "You're mad," Simeon spat. "The age of magicka is dead. The Queen's Inquisitors—" "The Queen's Inquisitors fear what they cannot bolt down and measure," Volkov interrupted. "They hanged Lady Blackwood for communing with shadows, yet here stands her daughter, reeking of the same dark gift." The truth of it made Aya's stomach clench. She'd hidden behind her mask, played at being a common thief, but blood would always tell. "Then you know I could be useful to you. My knowledge of the immateria—" "Was useful. Past tense, little sparrow." Volkov stood, her coat swirling like wings. "But you've shown your hand. Ambition without wisdom. Power without loyalty." "Please," Katra finally spoke, her usual bravado cracking. "We only wanted—" "I know what you wanted." Volkov's voice softened to something almost maternal, which was somehow worse. "The same thing they all want. To rise above your station. To taste power." She gestured to the writhing thing in its brass prison. "The Scythan lords understood that power demands sacrifice. Their entire civilization built on the backs of leviathans, their cities riding the great beasts through seas we can scarce imagine." Aya felt the immateria stirring unbidden in her blood, responding to the artifact's call. Dark whispers promised her escape, revenge, transformation—if only she would open herself fully to them. Just as her mother had. "I see it in your eyes," Volkov observed. "The hunger. The same look your mother wore in the dock at her trial." She turned to her bodyguards. "Take them below. The tide turns within the hour, and the deep channels will be hungry." As rough hands hauled them toward the deeper darkness, Aya caught Volkov's sleeve. "Wait. You speak of the old ways, of powers beyond steam and steel. I can help you understand them. My mother's journals, her research—" Volkov paused, considering. In that moment of hesitation, Aya glimpsed something almost like regret in the woman's scarred features. "Your mother was brilliant," Volkov said quietly. "And look where it led her." The foghorn sounded again in the distance, mournful and final.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully incorporating all requested story beats while maintaining the specified gritty Victorian aesthetic with gothic horror elements. The author effectively establishes the whalepunk setting through vivid details like whale vertebrae thrones, brass breathing apparatus, and mechanical legs with pistons. The characterization is particularly well-executed. Madame Volkov emerges as a compelling antagonist with clear motivations and a distinctive voice that balances maternal menace with calculated cruelty. Aya's internal struggle between her aristocratic heritage and criminal activities creates genuine tension, while her desperate attempts at negotiation feel authentic to her circumstances. The supporting characters, though less developed, serve their roles effectively. The dialogue carries authentic period flavor without feeling forced, with phrases like "God's bleeding wounds" and "steady on" that feel natural to the characters. Volkov's speeches about old powers versus mechanical progress effectively establish the world's philosophical tensions. The conversation flows organically while advancing both plot and character development. The prose demonstrates strong technical control with evocative imagery that serves the gothic atmosphere. Descriptions like "voice dripped like oil through gears" and "grin that belonged on a corpse" create visceral impressions without becoming overwrought. The pacing builds tension effectively, moving from the initial shock of discovery through the unmasking to the final desperate negotiation. The piece successfully avoids common pitfalls. There are no unearned character transformations or incongruent tonal shifts. The darkness feels well-earned given the criminal underworld setting and family tragedy. The ending maintains appropriate tension without false hope. Some minor weaknesses include occasional reliance on familiar fantasy tropes and moments where exposition slightly overwhelms action. The Scythan artifact explanation, while atmospheric, borders on being overly elaborate for the scene's needs.
