+ Iteration 2 — Avg Score: 80.6
+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 79.5
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The transfer vessel's cabin was a study in calculated minimalism—brushed steel walls broken only by the diagnostic screen's endless cascade of verdant text. Arthur Neegan pressed his palm against the viewport, watching Mars shrink to a rusty marble against the void. The metal was cold, almost painfully so, yet it grounded him in the present moment, away from the spiraling possibilities of what lay ahead. "Coffee, Mr. Neegan?" The steward—if that's what one called armed escorts in dress whites—held out a ceramic bulb with practiced steadiness despite the ship's subtle acceleration. "Black," Arthur said, accepting it gratefully. The warmth was welcome. "How long until we reach the rendezvous?" "Four hours, seventeen minutes." The steward's expression remained professionally neutral. "The Ambassador prefers punctuality." Ambassador. Arthur nearly laughed. The Antares Diplomatic Corps had about as much to do with diplomacy as a fusion bomb had to do with flower arranging. They were fixers, problem-solvers for problems that couldn't be solved through conventional channels. Problems like a mining engineer sitting on enough Azra to shift the balance of power in three star systems. The diagnostic screen flickered: PRESSURE NOMINAL. RADIATION SHIELDING 97.3% EFFICIENCY. AZRA CONTAINMENT FIELDS STABLE. That last line made Arthur's skin prickle. They were carrying Azra aboard. Not much—the energy signature was too weak for a commercial quantity—but enough for... what? A demonstration? Time dissolved into the hum of engines and the bitter tang of recycled air. When they finally docked, Arthur found himself in a reception room that could have been lifted from a twentieth-century luxury hotel, all mahogany panels and leather chairs. The anachronism was deliberate, he knew. Power often dressed itself in the clothes of antiquity. "Mr. Neegan." The voice came from a high-backed chair facing away from him. Female, cultured, with the faint lilt of the outer colonies. "Please, sit. We have much to discuss." Arthur circled to face her. She was younger than he'd expected, perhaps forty standard years, with prematurely silver hair pulled back in a severe bun. Her eyes were the color of Martian dust at sunset. "You know who I am," she said. It wasn't a question. "Lira Castellan. Deputy Director of Strategic Resources, Antares Sector." Arthur settled into the offered chair. "Though I suspect that's not why you're here." A smile played at the corner of her mouth. "Very good. Tell me, Mr. Neegan, what do you know about the Heisenberg Cascade?" "Theoretical physics isn't my field—" "Indulge me." Arthur frowned. "It's a hypothetical chain reaction in exotic matter. Under certain conditions, the quantum state of one particle can force neighboring particles into the same state, spreading exponentially until—" He stopped. "You're not talking about theory." "No." Castellan touched a control on her armrest. The room's lights dimmed as a holographic projection materialized between them—a three-dimensional molecular lattice that Arthur recognized immediately. Azra. "Your discovery isn't just the largest Azra deposit on record, Mr. Neegan. It's pure. Ninety-seven point six percent pure. Do you understand what that means?" "It means someone could weaponize it." "Someone already has." The projection shifted, showing a star system Arthur didn't recognize. One of the outer planets was missing a hemisphere, its exposed core glowing like a wound. "The Kepler Incident. Officially, a mining accident. Unofficially, the first field test of an Azra cascade weapon. Forty million dead." Arthur's coffee had gone cold. He set it aside with a trembling hand. "Why are you telling me this?" "Because in six days, the Martian Territorial Authority will revoke your mining claim under Eminent Domain provisions. In seven days, the Titan Consortium will challenge that revocation in court. In eight days, someone—we're not certain who—will attempt to take your site by force." Castellan leaned forward. "Unless you accept our offer." "Which is?" "Complete extraction of the Azra deposit. We have the technology to do it cleanly, safely, without triggering a cascade. The material will be secured, studied, and eventually destroyed. You'll be compensated, naturally. Enough to buy a small moon, if that's your preference." "And if I refuse?" "Then in nine days, Mr. Neegan, you'll be the richest dead man in the solar system." The diagnostic screen behind Castellan continued its serene scroll: LIFE SUPPORT OPTIMAL. HULL INTEGRITY 100%. ESTIMATED TIME TO MARS: 4:16:33. "You're asking me to trust the Antares Diplomatic Corps," Arthur said slowly. "An organization that officially doesn't exist." "I'm asking you to trust physics." Castellan stood, smoothing her grey suit. "That much pure Azra, in the wrong hands? It won't just be Kepler again. It'll be Earth. Luna. Every domed city from here to Proxima." She paused at the door. "You have six days to decide, Mr. Neegan. My steward will see you back to Mars." Alone, Arthur stared at the hologram still rotating in the air—his fortune, his curse, the future's knife-edge, all reduced to a dance of probability clouds and atomic bonds. Through the viewport, the stars watched with cold indifference, each one a reminder of how vast the universe was, and how small the margin for error. The diagnostic screen flickered once: AZRA CONTAINMENT FIELDS STABLE. For now.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully capturing Asimov's classic sci-fi style while delivering the requested scene structure. The author effectively establishes the retrofuturistic atmosphere through details like "diagnostic screen's endless cascade of verdant text" and the deliberately anachronistic reception room with "mahogany panels and leather chairs." The dialogue-heavy structure works well, with Lira Castellan emerging as a compelling antagonist who reveals information strategically. Her exposition about the Heisenberg Cascade and the Kepler Incident feels natural within the conversation while building the larger world and stakes. Arthur's responses show appropriate wariness and intelligence without being overly heroic. The world-building successfully captures Asimov's trademark approach of weaving large-scale political implications with intimate personal stakes. The mention of various factions (Martian Territorial Authority, Titan Consortium) and the timeline of escalating threats creates a sense of vast political machinery in motion. However, some elements feel slightly mechanical. The diagnostic screen updates, while atmospheric, border on heavy-handed symbolism. Certain phrases like "the future's knife-edge" veer toward purple prose. The pacing occasionally slows during exposition dumps, though these are generally well-integrated into the dialogue. The characterization is solid but not deeply nuanced. Arthur serves his function as the everyman caught in larger forces, while Castellan is effectively menacing but somewhat archetypal. The emotional stakes feel real but not deeply explored. The prose is generally elegant with good flow, though some sentences feel slightly overwrought. The ending maintains appropriate tension without false optimism, staying true to the noir-ish tone established throughout. + Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 85.0
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The red light makes everything feel like a secret. I lean against the counter, watching Elliot's silhouette as he gently rocks the developer tray. His humming—some indie song I can't place—vibrates through the small space, mixing with the chemical smell that's become weirdly comforting after all these late nights. "Twenty more seconds," I say, though we both know he's got the timing down perfectly. I just need to fill the silence before it fills me. He nods, silver rings catching the safelight as his fingers grip the tray edges. Those hands—I've been trying not to stare at them for months. Delicate but sure, black nail polish always slightly chipped. Tonight he's wearing this incredible mesh top under a oversized band tee I'm pretty sure he cropped himself, and I'm having the usual crisis about whether I want to photograph him or— "Dani?" His voice cuts through my spiral. "Can I tell you something?" My pulse kicks up. In six months of classes, Elliot's never initiated anything beyond project talk. I slide closer, careful to keep some space. My usual move would be to close that gap, let my shoulder brush his, but something about the way he's standing—coiled tight despite the soft edges—makes me hold back. "Always," I say, and mean it more than I expected to. He lifts the print, and we both watch the image emerge—a self-portrait where his face is multiplied and fractured through mirrors. It's gorgeous and unsettling, like everything he makes. "I've been thinking about... myself. Like, who I am." He clips the print up to dry, movements precise but trembling slightly. "Gender stuff." The darkroom suddenly feels smaller. I've navigated plenty of complicated attractions—the married professor, the straight girl who wasn't, the guy who wanted me to be softer than I'll ever be. But this feels different. Delicate, like the prints hanging around us. "Yeah?" I keep my voice neutral, fighting my instinct to take control of the conversation. Usually I'm the one leading, pushing, defining the terms. But Elliot's giving me something fragile here. He turns to face me, and the red light makes his eyes look like deep water. "I don't know if I'm... a guy. Not fully. Maybe not at all. I've been—" He laughs, sharp and nervous. "God, I've been writing this conversation in my head for weeks." "With me specifically?" The question slips out before I can stop it. His cheeks flush darker in the strange light. "You notice things. The way you photograph people... you see them. And you're so—" He gestures vaguely at all of me, and I can't help but grin. "So...?" "Yourself. Unapologetically yourself. I want that." I think about all the times I've watched him in class, the way he moves like he's apologizing for taking up space. How different it is from my own learned boldness, my carefully cultivated dominance. I've built my identity on knowing exactly what I want and taking it. But right now, what I want is to give him whatever he needs, and that uncertainty is thrilling and terrifying. "What pronouns feel right?" I ask, proud of how steady my voice sounds. "Still figuring that out." He—they?—start setting up the next print. "He is okay for now. But maybe... not forever?" I want to touch him so badly it aches. Not in my usual way—not the confident hand on a lower back, the strategic brush of fingers. I want to cup his face and tell him he's perfect in his uncertainty, that his questioning makes him more real than anyone I've known. But that's my desire talking, not what he needs. "Thank you for telling me," I say instead. "That's brave as fuck." He smiles then, real and unguarded, and it transforms his whole face. "I've been watching you all semester, you know. The way you move through the world like you own it. I thought maybe if I told you, some of that might rub off on me." "Elliot..." I start, but he's already turning back to the enlarger, adjusting the focus with shaking hands. "I know you usually go for... different types." His voice is carefully casual. "More masculine. More sure of themselves." And there it is—the thing I've been avoiding examining. My attraction to him doesn't fit my usual patterns. I like my partners defined, confident in their gender presentation whether that's masculine or feminine or beautifully both. But Elliot's uncertainty, his soft edges and questioning—it's rewriting something in me. "Maybe I'm figuring some things out too," I admit, and it feels like stepping off a cliff. He looks at me again, and there's something new in his expression. Not the shy interest I've caught glimpses of before, but something bolder. "Yeah?" "Yeah." I move closer, still leaving space for him to step back. "Turns out I really like people who hum while they work and wear better outfits than me and make art that keeps me up at night thinking." "That's very specific," he says, but he's leaning in now too. "I'm a very specific person." The timer goes off, sharp in the thick air between us. Neither of us moves to stop it. "I don't know what I want," he says quietly. "From this. From myself. From... anything." "That's okay," I tell him, and find that I mean it. "We can figure it out in the dark." His laugh is soft, barely there. "That was terrible." "I'm usually smoother." "No," he says, finally reaching for my hand. "This is better."
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional intelligence in handling a delicate subject matter. The narrative successfully captures Dani's perspective as requested, maintaining her assertive personality while showing vulnerability and growth. The darkroom setting is effectively utilized both literally and metaphorically, creating an intimate confessional atmosphere enhanced by the red light imagery. The characterization is particularly strong. Dani's voice feels authentic - confident yet uncertain when faced with new territory, maintaining her boldness while learning to step back and give Elliot agency. Elliot emerges as a fully realized character despite being seen through Dani's perspective, with genuine vulnerability and complexity in their gender questioning journey. The dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, avoiding exposition dumps while conveying necessary information about both characters' internal states. The conversation flows organically from tentative revelation to mutual vulnerability. The author handles the gender identity exploration with sensitivity and authenticity, avoiding both oversimplification and melodrama. The prose strikes a good balance between literary quality and accessibility. Sensory details like the chemical smell, humming, and red light create atmosphere without becoming overwrought. The internal monologue effectively reveals Dani's attraction and confusion without becoming repetitive. The pacing works well, building tension through small moments and gestures rather than dramatic revelations. The ending feels earned - both characters acknowledge uncertainty while finding connection, which feels realistic for their ages and situations. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose edges toward being slightly precious, and a few instances where Dani's thoughts could be more concise. The piece also relies somewhat heavily on the darkroom metaphor, though it generally works effectively. + Contemporary romance / drama: Stolen Moments in Roma — Score: 81.3
Prompt:
Contemporary Romance / Drama: Stolen Moments in Roma. Write a series of interconnected short scenes and dialogues that span a single day in modern-day Rome, in narrative form. This will form the beginning of a larger story. Alessandra, a fiery, independent Roman woman who works as a motorcycle courier, nearly collides with Ben, a somewhat clumsy, overly polite British academic on sabbatical, who is engrossed in a guidebook near the Trevi Fountain. The initial encounter is a clash of cultures and personalities – Alessandra is all sharp wit and rapid-fire Italian, while Ben is flustered apologies and awkward attempts at speaking the language. However, as they sort out the near-miss, a spark of unexpected humor and connection emerges. Alessandra, it quickly becomes clear, is a whirlwind of energy and mischief. She's a self-admitted troublemaker with a secret penchant for kleptomania, a detail she doesn't explicitly reveal, but which manifests in subtle, daring acts throughout the day. She impulsively decides to take Ben on a wild, unsanctioned tour of Rome, dragging him in some specific instances far outside his comfort zone. Ben, despite his initial apprehension and inherent politeness, finds himself utterly captivated by Alessandra's unapologetic spirit and zest for life. He is drawn into her orbit, a willing participant in increasingly outlandish situations, a quiet rebellion brewing beneath his scholarly exterior. The narrative should primarily consist of short, vivid scenes and dialogue exchanges, revealing their contrasting personalities and backgrounds, and the growing, ambiguous tension between them. Ben is captivated by Alessandra's energy and the thrill of breaking free from his routine. There's a clear sense that Alessandra is toying with Ben, pushing his boundaries and enjoying his reactions, but her own feelings remain somewhat enigmatic. The day should be a rollercoaster of exhilarating, slightly dangerous, and humorous moments, leaving Ben completely disoriented but utterly smitten. Explore the underlying tension & chemistry between them, culminating in some payoff as they part ways (or not -- your choice). Include this detail: Write in narrative prose, using immersive storytelling. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Stolen Moments in Roma** The August sun blazed mercilessly over the Trevi Fountain, transforming the piazza into a shimmering mirage of tourists and gelato vendors. Ben Whitmore, sweat pooling beneath his linen shirt, squinted at his guidebook, trying to decipher whether Bernini or Salvi deserved credit for the baroque masterpiece before him. "*Madonna!*" The screech of brakes jolted him backward. A cherry-red Vespa skidded to a halt inches from his loafers, its rider—a woman in ripped jeans and a leather jacket despite the heat—glaring at him through oversized sunglasses. "*Sei cieco?* Are you blind?" She yanked off her helmet, releasing a cascade of dark curls. "Standing in the middle of the street like a lamppost!" "I'm terribly sorry, I didn't—" Ben fumbled with his guidebook, which had somehow ended up on the cobblestones. "Mi dispiace, io sono—" "Stop." She held up a hand, switching to accented English. "Your Italian sounds like you're choking on pasta. British?" "Oxford, actually. Well, originally Surrey, but—" "*Dio mio*, you even apologize like a textbook." She revved the engine, then killed it, studying him with sudden interest. "What's so fascinating that you risk becoming roadkill?" Ben gestured weakly at the fountain. "I was reading about the architectural history—" Her laugh was sharp, musical. "You come to Roma to read about it? Like ordering wine to study the label." She swung off the Vespa in one fluid motion. "Alessandra." "Ben. Ben Whitmore." "Ben-Ben-Whitmore." She made his name sound like an incantation. "Tell me, professor—you are professor, yes? You have that look—when did books ever teach anyone to live?" --- Twenty minutes later, Ben found himself clinging to Alessandra's waist as she wove through traffic with suicidal confidence, his protests lost in the wind. She'd insisted on showing him "real Roma," which apparently began with stealing—borrowing, she'd corrected with a wink—an extra helmet from a nearby café. They screeched to a halt in Trastevere, where laundry fluttered between buildings like prayer flags. Alessandra dragged him through a maze of alleys, her fingers briefly lifting a flower from a vendor's cart as they passed. She tucked it behind her ear without breaking stride. "This is where Romans eat," she announced, pushing through the beaded curtain of a tiny osteria. "Not that tourist trap near your hotel." "How do you know where I'm staying?" "Please. Khakis, sensible shoes, guidebook? You scream Hotel Britannia." She ordered for them both in rapid-fire romanesco, the dialect washing over Ben like music he couldn't quite parse. The owner, a stooped man with knowing eyes, set down plates of *cacio e pepe* that made Ben's knees weak. "Better than your books?" Alessandra asked, twirling her fork with practiced ease. "I never said books were better than—" "Your face said it. All pinched and proper, like the world might break if you touched it wrong." She leaned forward, conspiratorial. "Tell me, Ben-Ben, when did you last do something without permission?" --- The afternoon dissolved into a blur of small rebellions. Alessandra led him through the Borghese gardens, where she picked the lock on a maintenance shed ("My cousin taught me") to retrieve a forgotten bottle of wine. They drank it beneath umbrella pines while she told him about her courier job, how she knew every shortcut and secret in the city. "Knowledge is power," she said, producing a pocket knife to open the wine. Ben noticed it had someone else's initials engraved on the handle. At the Pantheon, she showed him how to slip past the tourist crowds through a service entrance, emerging into the rotunda's sacred geometry. The afternoon light poured through the oculus like liquid gold. "Now close your eyes," she commanded. "Why?" "*Perché* I said so. Trust me." Ben obeyed, feeling ridiculous until her hand found his, warm and callused. She guided him to the center, directly beneath the opening. "Feel that? The weight of all this history, pressing down, but also..." She squeezed his hand. "The sky. Always the sky, reminding us not to take it so seriously." When he opened his eyes, she was watching him with an expression he couldn't read. --- "*Alessandra Conti!*" The voice cracked like a whip across the Piazza Navona. A well-dressed man approached, eyeing Ben with amusement. "Marco." Alessandra's smile didn't reach her eyes. "Still collecting strays?" Marco's English was smooth, diplomatic. He turned to Ben. "You should know, *amico*, our Alessandra has sticky fingers. Sticky everything, really. Hearts, wallets, whatever catches her fancy." "Marco's bitter because I kept his motorcycle after we broke up," Alessandra said sweetly. "You *stole* my motorcycle." "Semantics." Marco shook his head, addressing Ben. "Fair warning, professor. She'll take you for the ride of your life, then vanish with your favorite watch. Ask anyone in Trastevere." After he left, Alessandra's bravado flickered. "Romans love their drama," she said, but something had shifted. --- The sun was setting as they reached the Gianicolo hill. Rome spread below them like a Renaissance painting, all terracotta and gold. Alessandra sat on the wall, legs swinging over the edge. "Marco wasn't entirely wrong," she admitted. "I take things. Little things, usually. Stupid things." "Why?" She shrugged. "Started as a game when I was young. Now?" Another shrug. "Maybe I like proving nothing's permanent. Nothing's really owned." Ben surprised himself by laughing. "So this whole day—was I being stolen too?" "Maybe." She turned to him, sunset catching in her eyes. "Would that be so terrible?" He thought of his careful schedules, his annotated guidebooks, his life of permissions and propriety. "No. Not terrible at all." She smiled then, genuine and unguarded. "You're not what I expected, Ben-Ben-Whitmore." "Neither are you." They sat in silence as Rome darkened, the city's lights beginning their ancient dance. Finally, Alessandra stood, pulling something from her pocket—his university ID card, lifted hours ago without his noticing. "Souvenir?" she offered, grinning. "Keep it," Ben said. "But only if I can steal something in return." "*Bene*. What?" "Tomorrow. Let me show you my Rome. The boring, bookish parts you've been avoiding." She laughed, that sharp musical sound he was already addicted to. "You think you can make dusty libraries exciting?" "I think you could make anything exciting." The words hung between them, charged with possibility. Alessandra stepped closer, close enough that he could smell her perfume mixed with motor oil and sunshine. "Dangerous thinking, professor," she murmured. "I might actually take you seriously." She kissed him then, quick as a pickpocket, before mounting her Vespa. "Tomorrow, then. Try not to get run over before I corrupt you properly." She roared off into the Roman night, leaving Ben touching his lips, wondering if she'd stolen something after all—or if, perhaps, he'd finally learned to give it freely.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates the requested interconnected scenes spanning a single day in Rome, establishing clear character contrasts between Alessandra (fiery, independent Roman courier) and Ben (polite, clumsy British academic). The cultural clash is well-rendered through their initial encounter and subsequent interactions. The characterization is particularly effective. Alessandra emerges as a compelling figure whose kleptomania is woven naturally into the narrative through subtle actions (stealing the helmet, flower, wine opener with someone else's initials). Her complexity is revealed gradually - she's not just a manic pixie dream girl but someone with genuine depth and vulnerability, especially in the Marco confrontation and her honest admission about her stealing habit. Ben's transformation from buttoned-up academic to someone willing to take risks feels organic rather than forced. The dialogue crackles with authenticity, capturing Alessandra's sharp wit and Ben's awkward politeness effectively. The author skillfully uses code-switching between Italian and English to reinforce cultural differences while keeping the text accessible. Lines like "Your Italian sounds like you're choking on pasta" and "You even apologize like a textbook" establish character voice efficiently. The Roman setting is vividly rendered through specific locations (Trevi Fountain, Trastevere, Pantheon, Gianicolo hill) and sensory details that immerse the reader. The author demonstrates knowledge of the city while avoiding guidebook-style exposition. The pacing maintains momentum throughout, with each scene building tension and developing the relationship. The structure of short, interconnected vignettes works well for the "stolen moments" concept. The romantic tension builds naturally without feeling rushed or unearned. However, some elements feel slightly predictable - the "uptight academic meets free spirit" trope, while well-executed, doesn't break entirely new ground. The ending, while satisfying, follows expected romantic comedy beats. The prose occasionally veers toward the overly polished, though it generally maintains an appropriate tone. The piece succeeds in creating genuine chemistry between the characters and leaves the reader invested in their potential future. The writing demonstrates technical skill, emotional intelligence, and strong scene construction. + Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 79.8
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I keep wiping the same spot on the bar counter, watching the stranger's tentacles twitch with barely contained anxiety. The purple-tinged dreads writhe independently, a sure sign of Zelathi stress. In three years of bartending at The Last Outpost, I've learned to read the tells of every species that drifts through our little corner of nowhere. "Rough shift in the mines?" I ask, sliding the double Vega whisky across the worn synthteak bar. The amber liquid catches the soft glow of the nebula through the viewport behind me. The Zelathi's four eyes blink in sequence—surprise, then wariness. "How did you—" They stop, shake their head. "Not a miner." "Transport crew then? You've got that 'just survived an asteroid field' look." A bitter laugh escapes them. "Something like that." They knock back half the whisky in one go, tentacles curling tighter. "You're Nettle, right? Heard you're good at... discretion." I pause my wiping. Behind the stranger, my regular crowd fills their usual spots: Korvax and his crew playing gravity dice in the corner booth, Mira the salvage operator sweet-talking some newcomer by the jukebox, Old Henrik nursing his perpetual mug of Earthbrew. Normal Tuesday night stuff. "Discretion's part of the service," I say carefully. "Within reason." The stranger leans in, voice dropping. "I need to disappear for a while. Just a few cycles. Can you—" "Nets!" Korvax's gravelly voice cuts across the bar. "Another round when you get a chance!" I hold up a finger to the stranger—wait—and grab four bottles of Korvax's usual poison. As I pass their table, the big Draknari catches my wrist gently. "New friend seems jumpy," he rumbles low. "Want us to stick around?" Sweet of him to offer, but I've handled worse than nervous Zelathi. "All good, Kor. Thanks though." When I return, the stranger has finished their whisky. I pour another without being asked. "You were saying about disappearing?" They glance around, tentacles now practically knotted. "I'm Pix. I... I was supposed to deliver something. To Ganymede Station. But—" Pix stops, four eyes focusing on different parts of the room simultaneously. "But you didn't make the delivery?" "I couldn't! When I saw what was in the cargo..." Pix shudders. "Twenty-seven stasis pods. Kids, Nettle. Human kids, maybe eight or nine years old." My hand stills on the bar rag. The spot I've been wiping is practically gleaming. "You're telling me you were running trafficked children?" "I didn't know!" The words come out in a rush. "It was supposed to be mining equipment. Sealed cargo, triple hazard pay, no questions asked. But one of the pods malfunctioned during transit and—" Pix's voice cracks. "She looked just like my clutch-sister's youngling." I force myself to keep wiping, steady circular motions while my mind races. Child trafficking through the Outer Rim isn't unheard of, but it's been years since anyone was stupid enough to run that kind of cargo through our sector. Marshal Reeves cleaned house last time. "Where's the cargo now?" "Still on my ship. Docked at Bay Seven." Pix's tentacles unknot slightly. "I diverted here instead of Ganymede. Told them I had engine trouble, needed repairs. But they'll figure out—" The bar door chimes. Two humans in matching gray coveralls enter, scanning the room with practiced efficiency. Corporate security, from their bearing. My regulars don't even look up—we get all types here—but Pix goes rigid. "Bathroom's through the kitchen," I say quietly, tilting my head toward the back. "Emergency exit leads to the maintenance tunnels." Pix doesn't need to be told twice. They slip off the barstool and weave between tables, trying for casual and failing miserably. The security types track the movement, start forward. "Gentlemen!" I call out brightly. "Welcome to The Last Outpost. What can I get you?" They barely glance my way. "We're looking for a Zelathi. Purple dreads, about this tall." The taller one holds his hand at Pix's height. "Seen anyone like that?" I keep wiping that same damn spot. "We get lots of Zelathi through here. Mining crews mostly. You boys want a drink while you look around?" "This is official business," Shorter says, flashing a corporate badge too quickly for me to read the company name. "The individual we're seeking may be in possession of stolen property." "Stolen property." I taste the words. "Must be pretty valuable to send two of you all the way out here." Taller's jaw tightens. "Have you seen them or not?" Behind them, Korvax stands up, all two and a half meters of scaled muscle. Mira's hand drifts to her hip. Even Old Henrik sets down his mug. The temperature in my bar drops several degrees. I smile, sweet as Andromedan honey. "Like I said, we get lots of folks through here. You're welcome to wait around, see if your Zelathi shows up. First drink's on the house." The security types exchange glances. They've done the math—two of them versus a bar full of Rim dwellers who clearly don't like corporate badges. "We'll be back," Shorter promises. "With a warrant." "Looking forward to it." They leave, and the bar collectively exhales. I finally stop wiping that spot, toss the rag under the counter, and head for the kitchen. I've got a Zelathi to find and twenty-seven kids who need help. Just another Tuesday at The Last Outpost.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece successfully establishes a cozy sci-fi atmosphere while introducing meaningful conflict. The author creates a believable space bar setting with diverse alien species and maintains consistent first-person present tense narration throughout. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes - revealing character, advancing plot, and building tension. The worldbuilding is efficient and immersive, introducing concepts like Zelathi stress tells, various alien species, and the political dynamics of the Outer Rim without overwhelming exposition. The character of Nettle comes across as competent and experienced, with hints of her mysterious past that add depth without being heavy-handed. The central conflict involving child trafficking is serious and creates genuine stakes, while Pix's moral dilemma feels authentic. The arrival of corporate security provides immediate tension and sets up future complications effectively. The supporting characters like Korvax and the bar regulars feel lived-in and contribute to the community atmosphere. However, some elements feel slightly predictable - the mysterious cargo reveal follows expected beats, and the protective bar community rallying around Nettle is somewhat conventional. The pacing is generally good, though the transition from Pix's revelation to the security team's arrival feels slightly rushed. The prose is clean and functional, avoiding purple language while maintaining atmosphere. The dialogue achieves the requested 60% ratio and feels authentic to each character. The chapter ends on an appropriate cliffhanger without resolving the central conflict, as requested. The piece demonstrates solid craft in character voice, worldbuilding, and tension management, though it doesn't break significant new ground in terms of creativity or emotional complexity. + Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 82.2
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The strap slips again, and I hitch it back up for the millionth time since that trucker dumped me at the edge of the French Quarter at dawn. "End of the line, kid," he'd said, not unkindly, but firm. Now it's noon and the October heat is pressing down on me like Mom's disapproval, heavy and suffocating. I've never seen so many people who look like they're running from something. Or maybe that's just me projecting. Christ, I need to stop doing that. The smell hits different here—sweet rot and jazz and something alive that Memphis didn't have. I duck into a corner store for water, counting my cash. Eighty-seven dollars and change. The Pakistani guy behind the counter looks at me like he's seen a thousand kids like me walk through that door. Maybe he has. "You need something else?" His accent is thick but his eyes are knowing. "Just the water." My voice cracks. I've been trying to pitch it lower since I left, but exhaustion is winning. Outside, I nearly trip over a girl sitting against the wall, knees drawn up, purple-streaked hair falling over her face. She's maybe eighteen, nineteen. Leather jacket in this heat—insane or dedicated to an aesthetic. I respect both. "Watch it," she snaps, then looks up. Her eyes are lined with yesterday's eyeliner, smudged into something between goth and exhaustion. "Shit. Sorry. Thought you were a cop." I laugh, surprising myself. "Do I look like a cop?" "You look like you just fell off the turnip truck." She studies me, and I fight the urge to squirm. "First time?" "First time what?" "Running." The word hangs between us like a confession. I could lie, but what's the point? The strap slips again. "Yeah." She pats the ground next to her. "I'm Billie. Like the holiday, not the singer." "Taylor." I sit, grateful to drop the backpack. "Like... the tailor, I guess." "That's the worst introduction I've ever heard." But she's smiling. "How long you been gone?" "Day and a half." It feels like a year. It feels like five minutes. "Fresh meat," she says, but not mean. "Look, I'm gonna give you some free advice because you remind me of my brother. Don't trust anyone who offers you a place to stay tonight. Don't trust anyone who wants to 'help' you find work. And definitely don't trust anyone over thirty who seems too interested in your whole deal." My stomach turns. I hadn't thought that far ahead. Hadn't thought past *get out get out get out.* "Where do you sleep?" "Around. There's a few spots if you know where to look. Abandoned houses in the Bywater. Sometimes the levee if it's dry." She pulls out a crushed pack of cigarettes, offers me one. I shake my head. "Smart. These things'll kill you." "That's what my mom says." Said. Past tense now, probably. Billie's quiet for a moment. "They always do." A group of tourists passes, cameras and cruise ship lanyards, staring at us like we're part of the scenery. I want to scream at them. I want to disappear. I want to call home and say I'm sorry, take it all back, pretend I never said anything. But I can't unpeel that truth. Can't stuff it back inside where it lived for sixteen years, eating me alive from the inside out. "You okay?" Billie's watching me. "Yeah." No. "Just thinking." "That'll kill you faster than cigarettes." She stands, brushes off her jeans. "Come on. I'll show you where to get free food. But first—" She eyes my backpack, the broken strap, my everything. "You scream 'rob me.' We need to fix that." We walk deeper into the Quarter, past jazz bands and fortune tellers and people who look like they've made homes in the margins. Billie moves like she owns these streets, and maybe she does in a way. I try to copy her stride, her don't-fuck-with-me energy. "So what's your deal?" she asks finally. "Why'd you run?" The words stick in my throat. I haven't said it out loud since that night in the kitchen, Mom's face crumpling, Dad's voice rising. But something about Billie, about this whole impossible city, makes me brave. Or stupid. "I'm trans. Came out. It went... bad." She nods like it's nothing. Like it's everything. "Yeah. That'll do it." "What about you?" "Foster care lottery. Got tired of losing." She points to a building ahead. "That's the drop-in center. They do lunch til two. Don't give them your real name." "Why are you helping me?" She stops, really looks at me for the first time. There's something broken in her eyes that mirrors my own. "Because someone helped me once. And because—" She shrugs. "The street's hungry. It'll eat you if you let it. Maybe I'm just tired of watching kids get swallowed." The strap slips again. This time, I let it fall.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully capturing Taylor's voice as a 16-year-old trans runaway navigating their first day in New Orleans. The author effectively employs first-person mixed tense narration and incorporates both internal monologue and dialogue as requested. The characterization is particularly strong. Taylor feels authentically teenage - vulnerable yet trying to project toughness, overwhelmed but attempting to adapt. The internal contradictions ("I want to call home and say I'm sorry, take it all back" versus the inability to "unpeel that truth") ring true for someone in crisis. Billie emerges as a compelling secondary character with her own depth, avoiding the trap of being merely a plot device. The dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, with distinct voices for each character. Billie's street-smart cynicism ("Fresh meat," "The street's hungry") contrasts effectively with Taylor's more uncertain responses. The conversation flows organically and reveals character rather than simply conveying information. The prose strikes a good balance between accessibility and literary quality. Metaphors like "pressing down on me like Mom's disapproval" and "lived for sixteen years, eating me alive from the inside out" are effective without being overwrought. The sensory details of New Orleans - the heat, smells, sounds - ground the reader in the setting without becoming excessive. The emotional complexity is well-handled. Taylor's conflicted feelings about their situation, the mixture of fear and determination, and the complicated grief over their family situation all feel authentic. The piece avoids easy answers or unearned optimism while still providing moments of human connection. The pacing works well, moving from Taylor's immediate physical discomfort through the meeting with Billie to practical concerns about survival. The structure feels natural rather than forced, and the ending with the dropped backpack strap provides a subtle but meaningful moment of letting go. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the writing becomes slightly self-conscious, and a few descriptions that border on trying too hard. The piece also could have pushed deeper into Taylor's emotional state in places, though this may be appropriate given the character's likely emotional numbness. + Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 73.7
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**My Apocalypse Buddy** The morning sun cast long shadows through the skeletal remains of what had once been a thriving shopping district. Midas sat cross-legged on the floor of their makeshift shelter—a former frozen yogurt shop that still smelled faintly of artificial strawberry—staring at the day's objective: a pharmacy three blocks away that might, just might, still have antibiotics. "Right," said Midas to his companion. "Today's the day we finally get those meds for Old Pete. Simple in, simple out. What could possibly go wrong?" Chewy, a moth-eaten animatronic koala whose fur had seen better decades, swiveled his head with a mechanical whir. His glass eyes lit up with an eerie blue glow. "SATISFACTION GUARANTEED OR YOUR MONEY BACK!" he announced cheerfully. "Yes, thank you for that vote of confidence," Midas muttered, checking his backpack for the third time. Rope, check. Crowbar, check. Half a bottle of water and two suspicious-looking energy bars, check. "You know, when I imagined having a talking companion in the apocalypse, I pictured someone who could offer tactical advice, not corporate jingles from the before-times." "NEW AND IMPROVED FORMULA!" Chewy declared, raising one paw in what might have been enthusiasm or a malfunction. The journey to the pharmacy started well enough, which should have been Midas's first warning. They crept through the empty streets, avoiding the usual hazards: unstable buildings, roving packs of feral shopping carts, and the occasional crater where someone had tried to make coffee with pre-collapse energy drinks and industrial cleaning supplies. The pharmacy's automatic doors had long since seized up, frozen in a half-open position like a mouth caught mid-scream. Midas squeezed through, Chewy tucked under one arm. "Antibiotics, antibiotics," Midas whispered, scanning the ransacked shelves. Most of the good stuff had been taken in the first days after the Collapse, when people still thought hoarding toilet paper would save them. But there, behind a toppled display of vitamins that promised to make you "FEEL THIRTY YEARS YOUNGER!" (a claim that seemed particularly cruel now), he spotted a small cache of medicine bottles. That's when he heard it: the distinctive shuffle-thump-drag of a Scavenger. Scavengers weren't exactly human anymore. The Collapse had done strange things to people who'd been too connected to their devices when the networks went down. They wandered the ruins, still clutching their dead phones, occasionally trying to swipe right on passing survivors. "PRICES SO LOW, IT'S PRACTICALLY STEALING!" Chewy announced at maximum volume. The Scavenger's head snapped up, its hollow eyes fixing on them with predatory interest. "Really?" Midas hissed at the koala. "Now? You choose now for a sale announcement?" The chase that followed would have been comedic if it weren't so terrifying. Midas ran through the pharmacy like a contestant on a particularly dystopian game show, the Scavenger shambling after him with surprising speed. He vaulted over the pharmacy counter, sending a cascade of empty pill bottles rattling across the floor like plastic rain. "TRY OUR FAMILY PACK!" Chewy suggested helpfully as Midas dove behind a shelf. In his panic, Midas grabbed the wrong bottles—what he thought were antibiotics turned out to be a year's supply of pills designed to prevent hair loss in hamsters. Still, with a Scavenger bearing down on him, he didn't have time to be picky. The escape route he'd planned—back through the automatic doors—was blocked by two more Scavengers who'd been attracted by the noise. Midas did the only sensible thing: he threw Chewy at them. The koala sailed through the air, sensors activating mid-flight. "HELLO, VALUED CUSTOMER! WOULD YOU LIKE TO HEAR ABOUT OUR REWARDS PROGRAM?" The Scavengers stopped, transfixed by the talking toy. Their device-addled brains couldn't resist the siren call of a potential loyalty scheme. While they stood there, trying to remember their passwords, Midas scooped up Chewy and made his escape through a broken window, adding several new cuts to his growing collection. The journey back was a blur of wrong turns, close calls, and one unfortunate incident involving a nest of mutant pigeons that had developed a taste for synthetic fabrics. By the time they stumbled back into the frozen yogurt shop, Midas was bleeding from three different places, his jacket was missing a sleeve, and Chewy's left ear was hanging by a wire. "Well," Midas panted, collapsing onto his makeshift bed, "that went about as well as expected." He pulled out the bottles he'd risked his life for and squinted at the label. "Hamster... baldness... prevention. Fantastic." Chewy's head did a complete 360-degree rotation, a trick that never failed to unnerve. "BUT WAIT, THERE'S MORE!" "No," Midas groaned, covering his face with his hands. "There really, really isn't." Still, as the sun set over the ruins of civilization, Midas found himself patting the koala's matted head. In a world gone mad, even a malfunctioning toy spouting decades-old advertisements was better than facing the apocalypse alone. Tomorrow, he'd try again. Maybe the pet store would have antibiotics. Stranger things had happened. After all, he was taking survival advice from a mechanical koala. "Same time tomorrow?" he asked. "SUPPLIES LIMITED! ACT NOW!" Chewy replied. Midas chose to take that as a yes.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This story successfully captures the requested Terry Pratchett-inspired tone with dry humor and absurdist elements in a post-apocalyptic setting. The premise of a hapless survivor paired with a malfunctioning animatronic koala is genuinely creative and well-executed. The author demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt structure, beginning with mission planning, showing how things go wrong, and returning the characters home worse for wear. The characterization works well within the story's constraints. Midas is appropriately hapless and his reactions feel authentic to someone living in this bizarre world. Chewy's corporate jingles provide consistent comic relief while serving the plot. The voice remains consistently wry and observational throughout, with clever turns of phrase like "plastic rain" and "contestant on a particularly dystopian game show." The world-building is inventive, particularly the concept of Scavengers as device-addicted former humans and details like "feral shopping carts." The mission structure creates natural tension and pacing. The dialogue, while limited by Chewy's nature as a malfunctioning toy, serves its purpose effectively. However, the story has some weaknesses. The emotional depth is somewhat shallow - while the ending gestures toward themes of companionship and resilience, these feel underdeveloped. The prose occasionally relies on telling rather than showing, particularly in exposition about the world. Some descriptions border on overwrought, and certain plot elements feel predictable once established. The humor works well but sometimes overshadows character development. The relationship between Midas and Chewy, while charming, could be more nuanced. The story maintains good pacing but some transitions feel rushed, particularly the escape sequence. Overall, this is a competent piece that fulfills its brief with creativity and humor, though it doesn't quite achieve the depth or sophistication of Pratchett's best work. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 12 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Emotionally Complex: 10 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 7 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 15 Overall Impression: 15

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 78.8
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The bell above the door jangled frantically as someone burst through, bringing with them a gust of Welsh autumn air and the distant sound of shouting voices. "Bloody hell," the intruder muttered, pressing his back against the door. "Is there a back way out of here?" Bronwen looked up from her inventory clipboard, taking in the man in the expensive leather jacket and designer sunglasses who was currently using her door as a barricade. "The fire exit's alarmed. You'll have half of Llanfairpwll running if you use it." "Right. Brilliant." He peeled himself off the door and ducked behind the nearest bookshelf. "Mind if I just... browse for a bit?" "It's a bookshop. That's generally what people do here." She returned to her counting, though she couldn't help noticing how he kept glancing toward the windows. "You're not going to ask?" "Ask what?" "Why I'm hiding in your shop like some sort of literary fugitive." "I assumed you'd had a particularly traumatic experience at Waterstones." Despite himself, he laughed. "Oliver Rhys-Davies." He extended a hand, flashing the sort of smile that probably worked on everyone from baristas to BAFTA voters. "Bronwen." She shook his hand briefly and went back to her clipboard. "Just Bronwen? Like Cher?" "Bronwen Evans. Like someone who owns a bookshop and has stock to count." He pulled off his sunglasses, revealing startlingly green eyes that she definitely hadn't seen on several movie posters. Definitely not. "You really don't recognize me?" "Should I? Are you the new postman? Because Derek said he was retiring but—" "I'm an actor. Oliver Rhys-Davies? I was in that film about the spy who—" "The one who couldn't find his way out of a paper bag but somehow saved the world anyway? Saw the trailer. Looked very... explosive." "It grossed three hundred million worldwide." "I'm sure that's very nice for you." She moved to the next shelf, and he followed like a well-dressed shadow. "You didn't like it?" "I didn't see it. I don't really do films about men having mid-life crises with guns." "I'm thirty-two!" "The guns looked very shiny, though." He stared at her for a moment, then laughed again, more genuinely this time. "You're taking the piss." "Only a little." She allowed herself a small smile. "The poetry section's in the back corner if you need somewhere quieter to hide. No one ever goes there." "Poetry?" He perked up unexpectedly. "You have a good selection?" "Decent enough. Mostly Welsh poets, some classics. Why, do action stars read poetry between explosions?" "Actually..." He rubbed the back of his neck, and for the first time, the practiced charm flickered. "I studied English at Cambridge. Before all the... this." He gestured vaguely at himself. Bronwen raised an eyebrow. "Cambridge? And you ended up shooting fake guns at fake terrorists?" "The real guns wouldn't fit in my trailer." But his joke fell flat, and he wandered toward the poetry section she'd indicated. She tried to focus on her inventory, but found herself watching as he ran his fingers along the spines, his entire demeanor changing. The cocky swagger was gone, replaced by something almost reverent. "Oh, you have Gillian Clarke's collected works." His voice was different now, softer. "I met her once at a reading in Cardiff. Completely tongue-tied. Made a fool of myself." "Hard to imagine Oliver Rhys-Davies tongue-tied." "You'd be surprised. Put me in front of a camera with explosions, fine. Put me in front of someone who wrote 'Miracle on St David's Day' and I turn into a stammering undergraduate again." He pulled the book out carefully. "Do you know her work?" "'I am reading poetry to the insane,'" Bronwen quoted quietly. "'An old woman interrupting offers as many buckets of coal as I need.'" He looked up sharply. "You know it." "I know all of them. This is my shop." She abandoned her clipboard entirely, moving closer. "I have a first edition of 'Letter from a Far Country' in the back. Signed." "You're joking." "I don't joke about poetry." They stood there for a moment, the October light filtering through the windows, catching the dust motes between them. "I tried to option 'The Sundial' once," he admitted. "Wanted to adapt it. Everyone said poetry doesn't sell. Told me to stick to jumping out of helicopters." "Why didn't you?" "Because everyone loves the guy who jumps out of helicopters." The charm tried to reassert itself, but she could see through it now. "Oliver Rhys-Davies, action hero. It's a good gig." "Must be exhausting, being loved by everyone." "You'd think it would be enough, wouldn't you?" He was looking at her intently now, and she felt heat rise in her cheeks despite herself. The bell jangled again. "Bron, cariad, have you seen—Oh." Mrs. Pritchard from the post office stopped short. "You're that actor! From the film with the explosions! My granddaughter loves you!" Oliver's public smile snapped back into place like armor. "Always happy to meet a fan. Would she like an autograph?" As he charmed Mrs. Pritchard, Bronwen quietly retrieved the Gillian Clarke first edition from the safe in her office. When Mrs. Pritchard finally left, clutching her signed receipt, she held it out to him. "Borrow it," she said. "But I'll need collateral." "What kind of collateral?" "Come back when you've finished it. We'll discuss 'The Sundial.' Properly. No charm, no cameras, no adoring fans. Just the poetry." He took the book like it was made of spun glass. "You trust me with this?" "I trust anyone who can quote Gillian Clarke and mean it." She paused. "Even if they do jump out of helicopters for a living." "It was one helicopter. Okay, seventeen. But who's counting?" This smile was different—crooked, uncertain, real. "Next Tuesday? After closing?" "The shop closes at six." "I'll bring wine. Good wine. Poetry wine, not action hero wine." "I didn't know there was a difference." "Oh, there is. One comes with a corporate sponsor." She laughed despite herself, and his whole face lit up like she'd given him an award. "Tuesday," she confirmed. "Try not to bring any paparazzi with you." "I'll wear a disguise. Maybe a fake mustache." "That'll definitely help you blend in in Llanfairpwll." He clutched the book to his chest and gave her a little bow. "Until Tuesday then, Bronwen Evans. Just Bronwen." After he left, she stood in her quiet shop, surrounded by the familiar smell of books and possibility, and wondered what exactly she'd just agreed to.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt requirements, successfully creating a meet-cute scenario in a Welsh bookstore with well-defined character dynamics. The author effectively establishes Oliver as a charming but insecure actor whose usual tactics fail against Bronwen's immunity to celebrity, while Bronwen maintains a facade of disinterest that masks genuine attraction. The dialogue carries the scene effectively, with distinct voices for each character. Bronwen's dry wit and literary knowledge contrast nicely with Oliver's practiced charm that gradually gives way to vulnerability. The Welsh setting is authentically rendered through place names and cultural details without feeling forced. Character development shows genuine craft - Oliver's transformation from cocky celebrity to vulnerable poetry lover feels organic, triggered by Bronwen's resistance to his usual charm. The poetry connection provides a believable bridge between their worlds. Bronwen's gradual warming is subtly handled through small gestures and softening responses. The prose maintains an appropriate tone throughout, balancing humor with romantic tension. Descriptive elements are well-integrated without overwhelming the dialogue-driven narrative. The pacing builds naturally toward their arrangement to meet again. However, some elements feel slightly predictable - the celebrity hiding from paparazzi trope and the "immunity to charm creates attraction" dynamic are familiar romance beats. The resolution, while sweet, comes together perhaps a bit too neatly. Some of Oliver's vulnerability could have been earned more gradually. The writing demonstrates technical competence with natural sentence flow and effective scene construction. The emotional complexity is present but could be deeper - both characters' internal conflicts are somewhat surface-level despite being well-executed within those constraints. + Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 80.2
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The door groaned on whale-oil hinges as Aya pushed it open, revealing a chamber lit by the sickly green glow of phosphorescent lamps. The air hung thick with the scent of ambergris and rust, and something else—the metallic tang of fresh blood. "Well, well. The little magpie comes to steal from her own nest." Morgana Blackwater stood at the chamber's heart, very much not in the Crowguard's custody. The guild leader's scarred face split into a predator's smile as she leveled a brass-inlaid pistol at them. Behind her, three bruisers flanked the pedestal where the Scythan artifact should have rested—but didn't. *Fool, fool, fool.* Aya's mind raced as her hand instinctively moved toward the shadows at her belt, where the immateria always beckoned. But Morgana's eyes tracked the movement, and she tutted. "Ah-ah. None of your mother's tricks, girl. Yes, I know all about Lady Ashworth's talented daughter. Did you truly think a domino mask would hide you from me?" Morgana's free hand gestured lazily. "Briggs, if you would." The largest of the bodyguards moved with surprising speed. Aya tried to dodge, but Katra's sharp intake of breath behind her made her hesitate—just long enough for Briggs's meaty fist to connect with her temple. Stars burst across her vision as she crumpled. When the world stopped spinning, rough hands had already torn away her mask. The leather and silk fell to the grimy floor like a dead thing. "Lady Aya Ashworth." Morgana savored each syllable like aged wine. "The Viscount's own daughter, playing at being a common thief. Though hardly common, with those particular talents of yours." Simeon's pocket watch ticked like a metallic heartbeat. *Tick. Tick. Tick.* "This was all theater, then?" Simeon's voice carried its usual steadiness, though Aya heard the tremor beneath. "The arrest, the chaos above?" "My dear Mr. Crane, in this business, one must occasionally prune the ranks. Amazing what the desperate and ambitious reveal when they think opportunity knocks." Morgana's smile widened. "Though I confess, I hadn't expected to net quite such an exotic fish." Katra spat on the floor. "You used us as bait. Drew out every rat in the guild who'd make a play." "Clever girl. Pity you chose the wrong friends." Morgana nodded to her men, who moved to flank the trio. "On your knees. All of you." Cold steel pressed against Aya's neck as they were forced down. The stone floor bit through her trousers, sending chills up her spine. Her mind churned through possibilities, discarding each as quickly as it formed. The immateria whispered seductively, promising salvation if she'd only reach out and— "You hang me, you hang a Ashworth." The words tumbled out before Aya could stop them. "My father sits on the Lord Chancellor's council. Touch me, and you'll have every Crowguard in Morska tearing this place apart stone by stone." Morgana laughed—a sound like grinding gears. "Oh, sweet child. What makes you think I intend to hang you? No, no. A lord's daughter who can summon the dark things? Who bears the same taint that sent her mother to the gallows? You're worth far more alive." *Tick. Tick. Tick.* "The Admiralty pays handsomely for those who can commune with the leviathans," Morgana continued, circling them like a shark. "And failing that, the Brass Collective always needs test subjects for their automaton experiments. They do so love to see how immateria-touched flesh responds to mechanization." Aya's stomach lurched. She'd heard whispers of the Collective's experiments—human souls trapped in clicking, whirring prisons of brass and steel. "My father—" "Your father will receive a ransom note. Terribly tragic, his daughter kidnapped by unknown brigands. He'll pay, of course. And when he doesn't get you back..." Morgana shrugged. "Well, the canals are deep, and weighted bodies tell no tales." "You're making a mistake." Aya forced authority into her voice, though her hands trembled. "I know things. Guild secrets. The location of the Hightower cache. The identity of the Velvet Merchant." "All things I already know, pet." Morgana's pistol never wavered. "Though I am curious—what did you hope to accomplish with the artifact? Did you even know what it was?" *Tick. Tick. Tick.* Aya's mind seized on the question. A stall, however brief. "The Scythan Codex. A map to the breeding grounds of the ghost whales, where the barrier between our world and the immateria grows thin." "Half right." Morgana's eyes glittered. "It's not a map, girl. It's a key. One that opens doors better left closed. Doors your dear mother spent her final years trying to seal." The revelation hit like ice water. "You're lying." "Am I? Who do you think gave her to the Crowguard? She'd grown too dangerous, too willing to meddle with forces beyond her ken. Rather like her daughter." Morgana stepped closer, close enough that Aya could smell the whale-oil pomade in her hair. "Last chance, Lady Ashworth. Join me willingly, use those dark gifts in service to the guild, and your friends live. Refuse..." *Tick. Tick. Tick.* The watch had stopped.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully incorporating all requested story beats while maintaining the specified gritty Victorian aesthetic with gothic horror elements. The author effectively establishes the whalepunk setting through atmospheric details like whale-oil hinges, phosphorescent lamps, and ambergris scents, creating an immersive world where brass, oil, and steel dominate while magic remains feared and misunderstood. The dialogue feels authentic to the Victorian setting, with Morgana's predatory sophistication ("My dear Mr. Crane, in this business, one must occasionally prune the ranks") contrasting effectively with Katra's blunt pragmatism. The character voices are distinct and consistent throughout. Morgana emerges as a particularly well-crafted antagonist—calculating, informed, and genuinely threatening without resorting to cartoonish villainy. The pacing builds tension effectively, moving from the initial shock of discovery through the unmasking to Aya's desperate attempts at negotiation. The revelation about Aya's mother adds emotional complexity and raises the stakes beyond mere physical danger. The author skillfully weaves Aya's internal monologue with external action, showing her desperation and quick thinking under pressure. The prose maintains an appropriate gothic atmosphere without becoming overwrought. Descriptions like "scarred face split into a predator's smile" and "sound like grinding gears" reinforce both the mechanical aesthetic and threatening tone. The recurring motif of Simeon's pocket watch provides an effective tension device that culminates meaningfully. However, some elements feel slightly predictable—the "it was all a trap" revelation and the mentor figure having betrayed the protagonist's parent are familiar tropes. The piece also relies heavily on exposition through dialogue, particularly Morgana's explanations about the artifact and Aya's mother, though this serves the story's needs adequately. The emotional complexity is well-handled, particularly Aya's realization about her mother's fate and her struggle between using her dangerous powers and protecting her friends. The ending maintains appropriate darkness without feeling gratuitously grim, setting up future conflict effectively. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
